proposals.title: Functional Characterization of Marine Protein Hydrolysates: Correlating Peptide Profiles with Emulsifying, Foaming, and Water-Binding Properties for Food Applications
proposals.motivation: While the antioxidant potential of protein hydrolysates from marine and plant sources is well-documented, their broader functional properties critical for food formulation remain underexplored. There is a growing industrial demand for natural, multifunctional ingredients to replace synthetic additives, necessitating a comprehensive evaluation of hydrolysates beyond bioactivity. This research directly addresses the need to valorize underutilized marine byproducts by assessing their techno-functional performance in food systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific peptide profiles, molecular weight distributions, and surface-active amino acid compositions generated during enzymatic hydrolysis of marine proteins will directly govern their emulsification, foaming, and water-binding capacities. Furthermore, we posit that these functional properties can be modulated by hydrolysis conditions independent of, or complementary to, antioxidant activity.
proposals.method: Marine proteins (e.g., from fish processing byproducts) will be hydrolyzed using food-grade enzymes (e.g., Alcalase, Flavourzyme) under controlled conditions of time, temperature, and pH to generate hydrolysates with varied degrees of hydrolysis. Peptide profiles will be characterized via UPLC-MS/MS and size-exclusion chromatography. Functional properties will be assessed using standard methods: emulsifying activity and stability index, foam capacity and stability, and water-holding capacity. Statistical analysis (e.g., PCA, multiple regression) will correlate peptide characteristics with functional performance metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed functional map of marine protein hydrolysates, identifying key hydrolysis parameters for tailoring ingredients for specific food applications like sauces, baked goods, or meat analogs. It will establish structure-function relationships between peptide characteristics and techno-functional properties, offering a scientific basis for the targeted use of hydrolysates as multifunctional food ingredients. The findings will expand the valorization strategy for marine biomass beyond nutraceuticals into structural food components.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Shahidi's prior work has extensively characterized the phenolic and antioxidant profiles of various sources, this proposal shifts focus to the under-investigated techno-functional performance of protein hydrolysates themselves. The novelty lies in systematically linking specific hydrolysate peptide fingerprints, obtained via advanced UPLC-MS/MS, directly to measurable physical functionalities relevant to food manufacturing, a holistic approach not commonly taken in prior antioxidant-centric studies.

proposals.title: Thermal Processing of Marine Protein Hydrolysates: Differential Effects on Phenolic Compound Release, Degradation, and Antioxidant Capacity
proposals.motivation: Thermal processing is a critical step in food production, but its impact on bioactive compounds is often oversimplified as a net loss. This assumption overlooks the potential for thermal energy to disrupt food matrices, thereby increasing the extractability and bioaccessibility of bound phenolics. A nuanced understanding of these competing effects (degradation vs. release) is essential for optimizing the nutraceutical potential of underutilized marine and plant resources, aligning with the global trend towards valorizing food byproducts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled baking of marine protein hydrolysates and fruit byproduct-enriched matrices will cause the selective degradation of certain free phenolic acids (e.g., gallic acid) while simultaneously liberating bound phenolic compounds from the protein-phenolic complex, leading to a shift in phenolic profile and a non-linear change in overall antioxidant capacity.
proposals.method: Marine protein (e.g., from shrimp or fish) hydrolysates will be combined with phenolic-rich fractions from underutilized sources like Jerivá or Butiá. Model systems will undergo controlled baking at 150-200°C for varying times. UPLC-MS/MS, as used in prior work, will quantitatively track specific phenolic compounds (e.g., gallic acid, pterostilbene analogs). Antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH) and protein-phenolic binding (via fluorescence quenching and SDS-PAGE) will be measured pre- and post-treatment. Statistical modeling will correlate thermal parameters with compound-specific degradation/release kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, compound-specific framework for understanding thermal effects on protein-phenolic systems, moving beyond the 'net loss' paradigm. It will deliver practical processing guidelines to maximize the retention and bioaccessibility of antioxidants in functional food ingredients, directly contributing to the nutraceutical valorization of marine and agri-food byproducts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly testing and decoupling the two opposing thermal mechanisms—degradation and matrix release—within the same protein-phenolic system, an inversion of the common stability assumption. This approach integrates Shahidi's expertise in marine hydrolysates, phenolic antioxidants, and underutilized fruit bioactives into a single, hypothesis-driven thermal processing study.

proposals.title: Application of Subcritical Water-Extracted Jujube (Ziziphus jujuba Mill.) Phenolics as a Natural Antioxidant in Meat Model Systems: Efficacy Against Lipid and Protein Oxidation
proposals.motivation: The food industry seeks clean-label, natural antioxidants to replace synthetic ones like BHA and BHT. Subcritical water (SCW) extraction is a green, efficient method to recover phenolic antioxidants from agri-food byproducts, such as jujube, but their efficacy is typically validated only in chemical assays. There is a critical knowledge gap regarding their performance in complex, real-world food matrices where interactions with lipids and proteins can diminish activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the optimized SCW extract from jujube, rich in identified phenolic acids and flavonoids, will effectively inhibit lipid peroxidation and protein carbonyl formation in comminuted meat systems, with its efficacy comparable to or greater than a common synthetic antioxidant at equivalent phenolic concentrations.
proposals.method: The optimized SCW extract from jujube pomace, previously characterized via UPLC-MS/MS for phenolic profile (e.g., chlorogenic acid, rutin) and ORAC/FRAP capacity, will be incorporated into a raw, lean ground pork model system at two concentrations (100 and 200 ppm gallic acid equivalents). Treatments will include a control (no antioxidant), SCW extract treatments, and a 200 ppm butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT) positive control. Samples will be stored at 4°C for up to 10 days. Lipid oxidation will be monitored by measuring thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) every 2 days. Protein oxidation will be assessed by quantifying protein carbonyls using the 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) method at days 0, 5, and 10. Statistical analysis will be performed using ANOVA with Tukey's test.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide crucial translational data on the effectiveness of a green-extracted nutraceutical in a biologically relevant food system, moving beyond model chemical assays. It will establish a direct link between the extract's characterized phenolic profile and its functional performance in inhibiting both major pathways of food quality deterioration (lipid and protein oxidation), supporting the valorization of jujube byproducts.
proposals.novelty: While SCW extraction of phenolics is established, applying a fully characterized SCW jujube extract specifically to mitigate protein oxidation in meat, in addition to lipid oxidation, represents a novel, holistic approach to food preservation that aligns with Dr. Shahidi's expertise in marine protein oxidation and fruit phenolics.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Mechanisms of Phenolic Compound Enhancement in Marine Protein Hydrolysates and Underutilized Fruit Byproducts Following Thermal Processing
proposals.motivation: Thermal processing, such as baking, is known to paradoxically increase the measurable phenolic content and antioxidant capacity of certain food matrices, yet the underlying chemical and biochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. This phenomenon is of significant interest to the nutraceutical and functional food industries, as it could lead to strategies for enhancing the bioactive potential of protein-rich marine hydrolysates and underutilized fruit byproducts, aligning with pre-2026 trends in valorizing waste streams and improving food quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed increase in phenolic content post-baking is primarily driven by (1) the thermal liberation of bound phenolic compounds from macromolecular complexes (e.g., proteins, dietary fiber) and (2) the Maillard reaction-induced formation of novel phenolic-like compounds with enhanced extractability and antioxidant properties.
proposals.method: The study will employ a model system combining marine protein hydrolysates (e.g., from shrimp or fish) with phenolic-rich byproducts from jerivá or butiá fruit processing. Samples will undergo controlled baking at varying temperatures and times. Phenolic profiles will be meticulously characterized using UPLC-MS/MS to identify specific compounds (e.g., gallic acid derivatives) and track transformations. Parallel analyses will include quantification of total phenolics, antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH), and monitoring of Maillard reaction products (e.g., furosine, advanced glycation end-products) to correlate chemical changes with bioactivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic explanation for the thermal enhancement of phenolics, moving beyond mere observation to causal understanding. It will directly contribute to the optimization of thermal processing parameters for maximizing the nutraceutical potential of marine and fruit-based ingredients. The findings will offer a scientific basis for developing novel, value-added functional food ingredients with tailored antioxidant profiles.
proposals.novelty: While increases in phenolic content after heating have been noted, this proposal uniquely integrates Shahidi's expertise in marine proteins and underutilized fruit phenolics to systematically deconvolute the contribution of release from macromolecular binding versus neo-formation via the Maillard pathway, a distinction rarely investigated in combined matrices.

proposals.title: Optimization of Subcritical Water Extraction for Targeted Recovery of Chlorogenic and Ferulic Acids from Marine Byproducts Using Response Surface Methodology
proposals.motivation: Subcritical water extraction (SCW) is a promising green technique for recovering bioactive compounds from food matrices, but optimization typically targets total phenolic yield, not specific high-value acids. Chlorogenic and ferulic acids are potent antioxidants with demonstrated nutraceutical and health-promoting potential, as evidenced by pre-2026 research on phenolic compounds. Efficient, targeted recovery of these individual acids from underutilized marine protein hydrolysate byproducts aligns with the sustainable valorization of waste streams, a key pre-2026 research theme.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically modeling SCW extraction parameters (temperature, pressure, time, and solid-to-solvent ratio) using Response Surface Methodology (RSM), we can identify distinct, optimized conditions that maximize the individual yield and antioxidant potency of chlorogenic acid and ferulic acid from marine-derived protein processing residues.
proposals.method: The method will employ a central composite design within RSM to model and optimize four independent SCW variables: temperature (100-200°C), pressure (10-50 bar), extraction time (5-30 min), and solid-to-solvent ratio. Extracts from marine byproducts (e.g., shrimp shell or fish frame hydrolysates) will be analyzed via UPLC-MS/MS for precise quantification of chlorogenic and ferulic acids, and their antioxidant capacity will be assessed using DPPH and ORAC assays. The desirability function will be used to identify optimal conditions for each target compound.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a precise, data-driven protocol for the selective extraction of specific phenolic acids, moving beyond generic total phenolic content optimization. It will contribute to the valorization of marine byproducts by identifying their specific nutraceutical potential. The generated models will offer a methodological framework applicable to other bioactive compounds in food and marine systems.
proposals.novelty: While SCW and RSM have been combined for total phenolics, the targeted optimization for individual, structurally distinct phenolic acids (chlorogenic vs. ferulic) from marine protein hydrolysate matrices is a novel and non-obvious application, requiring the identification of compound-specific extraction windows rather than a single global optimum.

proposals.title: Co-Optimization of Extraction and Thermo-Resilience: A Targeted Strategy for Upcycling Marine and Tropical Fruit By-Products into Stable Antioxidant Ingredients
proposals.motivation: The food industry generates massive quantities of by-products rich in phenolic antioxidants, representing a significant opportunity for sustainable valorization. However, the functional application of these extracts is often limited by their degradation under thermal stress during processing and storage, leading to unpredictable shelf-life and loss of bioactivity. A systematic approach linking extraction parameters directly to the thermal stability of the recovered compounds is critically needed to transform waste streams into reliable, high-value ingredients.
proposals.hypothesis: Extraction protocols (varying solvent systems, time, and methods like enzymatic hydrolysis) can be specifically tuned to co-optimize for both high phenolic yield and enhanced thermo-resilience of the antioxidant compounds recovered from food by-products, and this stability can be predictively modeled under dynamic, non-isothermal conditions.
proposals.method: Marine (e.g., shrimp shell, fish frame) and plant (e.g., jerivá pomace, seed cakes) by-products will be subjected to extraction using water, ethanol, and their acidified mixtures, alongside enzymatic hydrolysis to generate protein hydrolysates and release bound phenolics. Extracts will be characterized for total phenolic content, specific compounds (e.g., gallic acid, pterostilbene analogs) via UPLC-MS/MS, and antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH). Thermo-resilience will be assessed by subjecting extracts to non-isothermal stability studies mimicking supply chain temperature fluctuations, with degradation kinetics modeled using the Arrhenius equation and Weibull distribution to predict shelf-life.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, targeted methodology for producing antioxidant ingredients from waste with predictable performance under thermal stress. It will establish direct structure-stability relationships between extraction conditions and the resilience of specific phenolic profiles, offering practical guidelines for industry. The research expands the nutraceutical potential of underutilized sources by addressing a key barrier to their commercial application.
proposals.novelty: While extraction for yield is well-studied, this research uniquely integrates extraction optimization with systematic, kinetic-based stability assessment under realistic, non-isothermal conditions. It moves beyond static antioxidant assays to functionally link sustainable sourcing parameters directly to the thermo-resilient performance of the final ingredient.

proposals.title: Inverted Stability Testing: Quantifying the Primary Antioxidant Efficacy of Marine Protein Hydrolysate Phenolics in a Pure, Oxidation-Prone Lipid System
proposals.motivation: Evaluating antioxidant efficacy in pre-blended, inherently stable lipid matrices can obscure the true protective capacity of bioactive extracts due to confounding synergistic effects. This complicates the accurate determination of minimum effective concentrations and the isolation of primary mechanisms. A method adapted from corrosion inhibition testing, using a pure, unstable lipid as a high-stress baseline, is needed to address this gap in food and nutraceutical lipid stabilization research.
proposals.hypothesis: Testing a marine protein hydrolysate phenolic extract in pure, highly unsaturated wheat germ oil will reveal its fundamental antioxidant efficacy and non-linear concentration-response relationships more accurately than testing in a pre-mixed wheat germ/almond oil blend. Incremental addition of stable almond oil after primary testing will then elucidate specific synergistic or dilution effects on oxidative stability.
proposals.method: The method begins by creating a high-stress baseline: pure wheat germ oil will be spiked with varying concentrations of a characterized phenolic extract from blue mussel (Mytilus edulis) protein hydrolysates. Oxidative stability under accelerated conditions (Rancimat, 110°C) and formation of primary (peroxide value) and secondary (p-anisidine value) oxidation products will be monitored. Only after establishing this dose-response curve will refined almond oil be added incrementally to the most effective concentration, and stability re-tested. All extracts will be characterized via UPLC-MS/MS for phenolic acid and other antioxidant profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for isolating the primary antioxidant action of bioactive extracts from complex food matrices. It will deliver precise data on the efficacy of underutilized marine phenolics in lipid systems and establish a clearer paradigm for identifying true synergies in oil blends, directly informing the development of more effective natural preservatives.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard practice of testing antioxidants in pre-formulated blends by adopting a 'corrosion inhibition' analogy, starting with a pure, unstable substrate. This isolates the extract's intrinsic activity, a strategy not previously applied in the evaluation of marine or fruit byproduct antioxidants for lipid stabilization.

proposals.title: Interfacial Rheology of Marine Protein-Phenolic Hydrolysate Complexes: A Mechanistic Link to the Bioaccessibility and Antioxidant Activity of Phenolics in Emulsions
proposals.motivation: The bioaccessibility of dietary phenolics from underutilized sources, such as marine byproducts and tropical fruits, is often limited by their instability during digestion. While emulsions are effective delivery systems, current research focuses primarily on physical stability and gross antioxidant capacity, neglecting how the specific mechanical properties of the interfacial layer govern phenolic release kinetics and metabolic fate. Establishing a direct link between interfacial rheology and bioaccessibility is crucial for rationally designing functional foods with optimized health benefits.
proposals.hypothesis: The interfacial viscoelasticity of oil-water emulsions, modulated by complexes formed between marine protein hydrolysates and phenolic extracts from sources like jerivá or butiá, directly controls the rate and extent of phenolic release during digestion, thereby dictating their compartment-specific antioxidant activity and bioaccessibility.
proposals.method: Marine protein (e.g., from shrimp or fish processing waste) hydrolysates will be complexed with phenolic extracts from tropical fruits (e.g., jerivá) or their byproducts. These complexes will stabilize model emulsions. Interfacial rheology (dilatational and shear moduli) will be characterized using a pendant drop tensiometer with oscillatory deformation and a double-wall ring geometry rheometer. Emulsions will undergo a standardized INFOGEST 2.0 simulated gastrointestinal digestion. Phenolic release profiles, bioaccessibility (micellar fraction), and antioxidant activity (ORAC, DPPH) will be measured in gastric and intestinal phases and correlated with the initial interfacial rheological parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework linking the interfacial mechanical properties of protein-phenolic stabilized emulsions to specific digestive outcomes for phenolics. It will advance the field from empirical formulation to mechanistic design, enabling the targeted delivery of antioxidants. The findings will directly apply to valorizing underutilized marine and plant resources for nutraceutical applications.
proposals.novelty: While interfacial rheology is studied for physical stability, its direct role in programming the bioaccessibility and digestive-tract antioxidant activity of phenolics is unexplored. The fusion of Shahidi's expertise in marine protein hydrolysates and underutilized fruit phenolics with advanced colloidal digestion models creates a novel, mechanistic approach to functional emulsion design.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled Human Intervention Trial to Validate the Anticancer Mechanisms of Marine Protein Hydrolysate-Derived Phenolic Compounds on Oxidative Stress and Inflammatory Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive in vitro and animal model evidence for the anticancer potential of phenolic compounds from marine and plant sources, a critical translational gap exists. Long-term human intervention data confirming the proposed mechanisms, such as sustained antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, are lacking. Validating these mechanisms in humans is essential for developing evidence-based dietary strategies and functional foods for cancer risk reduction.
proposals.hypothesis: Long-term dietary supplementation with a characterized phenolic-rich extract from marine protein hydrolysates will significantly modulate key anticancer mechanisms in healthy at-risk adults, as evidenced by sustained reductions in systemic oxidative stress (e.g., F2-isoprostanes) and pro-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α) compared to a placebo.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial with 120 participants at elevated risk for colorectal cancer. The intervention group will receive a daily dose of a standardized phenolic extract derived from enzymatically hydrolyzed marine by-products (e.g., shrimp, salmon frames), previously characterized via UPLC-MS/MS for compounds like gallic acid and pterostilbene analogs. Primary outcomes will be changes from baseline in plasma F2-isoprostanes (oxidative stress) and serum IL-6/TNF-α (inflammation), measured at 0, 3, and 6 months. Secondary outcomes will include changes in antioxidant enzyme activities (SOD, GPx) and metabolomic profiling of phenolic metabolites.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first long-term human data directly linking the consumption of a defined marine-derived phenolic complex to mechanistic biomarkers relevant to cancer prevention. It will bridge the gap between preclinical chemoprevention studies and human nutritional epidemiology, offering robust evidence for the bioactive potential of underutilized marine resources in functional food development.
proposals.novelty: While human trials on fruit phenolics exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on a characterized phenolic profile from marine protein hydrolysates—a sustainable by-product stream—and employs a comprehensive biomarker panel over a longer duration to capture sustained mechanistic effects not assessed in short-term studies.

proposals.title: Scale-Up of Subcritical Water Extraction for High-Yield Recovery of Phenolic Compounds from Marine Protein Hydrolysate Byproducts
proposals.motivation: The nutraceutical industry's demand for natural antioxidants like phenolic compounds is growing, but conventional solvent extraction is not environmentally sustainable or cost-effective for large-scale production. Marine processing byproducts, such as those from fish protein hydrolysate production, are rich in phenolic-protein complexes but are underutilized due to a lack of scalable, green extraction technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subcritical water extraction (SWE), a solvent-free and scalable green technology, can be optimized to efficiently recover phenolic compounds with high antioxidant capacity from marine protein hydrolysate byproducts, yielding extracts suitable for commercial nutraceutical applications.
proposals.method: The research will optimize SWE parameters (temperature: 120-200°C, pressure: 10-50 bar, time: 10-40 min) for phenolic yield from defatted marine hydrolysate solids. The process will be scaled from lab (0.5 L vessel) to pilot (10 L vessel) scale. Extracts will be characterized for total phenolic content (Folin-Ciocalteu), antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH), and specific phenolic acid profiles (e.g., gallic, protocatechuic) via UPLC-MS/MS. The protein-phenolic interactions and bioaccessibility will also be assessed.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, scalable green extraction protocol for industry, transforming low-value marine byproducts into high-value phenolic ingredients. It will advance the fundamental understanding of SWE's efficacy on protein-bound phenolics and provide concrete data on the nutraceutical potential of a sustainable marine resource.
proposals.novelty: While SWE has been studied for plant materials, its systematic scale-up and application for valorizing phenolic compounds from marine protein hydrolysate byproducts—a complex matrix—is novel and directly addresses a critical gap in industrial green processing.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Resolution of Phenolic Profiles in Marine and Underutilized Plant Tissues: Linking Microscale Composition to Bioactive Potential
proposals.motivation: Current analyses of phenolic compounds in food and marine sources, including hydrolysates, rely on bulk tissue extracts, masking critical heterogeneity at the cellular level. This obscures the true localization of potent antioxidants and bioactive phenolics, limiting our understanding of their biosynthesis, storage, and functional roles within specific plant or algal tissues. Resolving this is key to optimizing the extraction and nutraceutical application of these compounds from underutilized sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant, functionally relevant heterogeneity in phenolic composition and antioxidant capacity exists at the single-cell and tissue-type level within marine and underutilized plant matrices, which is not captured by conventional bulk analysis.
proposals.method: We will employ laser capture microdissection (LCM) to isolate specific cell types (e.g., epidermal, mesophyll, seed coat) from marine algae and underutilized fruits (e.g., Jerivá, Butiá). Single-cell and micro-tissue phenolic extracts will be analyzed using nanoflow ultra-performance liquid chromatography (nanoUPLC) coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS), building on established UPLC-MS/MS protocols but scaled down. Data will be processed using multivariate statistical analysis (PCA, PLS-DA) to map phenolic distribution and correlate specific profiles with antioxidant activity measured via microplate-based ORAC and DPPH assays on parallel samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first spatially resolved phenolic maps for selected marine and underutilized plant tissues, directly linking micro-scale chemistry to bioactivity. It will establish a novel analytical pipeline for single-cell phytochemistry, offering deeper insight into plant defense mechanisms and creating a knowledge base for targeted bioprocessing to enrich specific bioactive fractions from food byproducts.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell 'omics' are emerging in biology, their application to food phytochemistry and nutraceutical research, particularly for phenolic compounds in complex marine and plant tissues, is unprecedented. This bridges Shahidi's expertise in bulk phenolic analysis with cutting-edge spatial metabolomics.

proposals.title: Pro-Oxidant Activation of Marine Protein Hydrolysate-Derived Phenolics: A Dual-Function Strategy for Chemoprevention and Targeted Cancer Therapy
proposals.motivation: Phenolic compounds from food and marine sources are widely studied for their antioxidant and chemopreventive properties. However, their potential to act as pro-oxidant 'pro-drugs' under specific cellular conditions, particularly in cancer cells with elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS), remains underexplored for therapeutic application. This dual-function paradigm could bridge the gap between nutritional prevention and targeted pharmacological intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific phenolic compounds, particularly those derived from marine protein hydrolysates, can exhibit a context-dependent dual role: acting as antioxidants in normal cells for prevention, while functioning as pro-oxidant agents in the tumor microenvironment to induce selective cytotoxicity and overcome drug resistance.
proposals.method: We will extract and fractionate phenolic compounds from marine protein hydrolysates (e.g., from shrimp or fish processing byproducts) using established protocols. Fractions will be screened for antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH) and pro-oxidant potential (metal chelation, ROS generation in cell-free systems). Using UPLC-MS/MS, we will identify lead phenolic acids and other phenolics. Their dual function will be tested in vitro using renal carcinoma cell lines (e.g., 786-O, ACHN) with established sunitinib resistance, comparing effects in cancer versus normal kidney cells. We will measure intracellular ROS, apoptosis (via caspase-3/7 activity), and key resistance pathway markers (RLIP76, p-ERK, p-Akt) by western blot.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel functional link between the chemopreventive and pro-oxidant therapeutic roles of food-derived phenolics. It will identify specific phenolic candidates from underutilized marine sources with dual-action potential, providing a mechanistic rationale for their use in adjuvant therapy to combat drug resistance in cancers like renal carcinoma.
proposals.novelty: While phenolics are known for antioxidant activity, systematically leveraging their pro-oxidant switch in a targeted therapy context, especially using phenolics from marine protein hydrolysates—a rich source identified in Shahidi's prior work—is a novel approach. This moves beyond mere antioxidant characterization into a deliberate pro-drug design strategy.

proposals.title: Mapping the Non-Linear Degradation of Fortified Phenolics in Marine Protein Hydrolysates Under Dynamic Temperature Stress: A Pathway to Off-Flavor Formation and Quality Loss
proposals.motivation: Fortification of foods like marine protein hydrolysates with phenolic antioxidants is a key strategy to enhance their nutraceutical value. However, the degradation of these added phenolics under non-isothermal, real-world storage conditions is poorly understood, leading to unpredictable losses in bioactivity and the potential formation of undesirable sensory compounds. This research addresses a critical gap in ensuring the stability and consumer acceptability of functional foods throughout the supply chain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fluctuating storage temperatures will catalyze the non-linear degradation of specific fortified phenolics (e.g., gallic acid, pterostilbene analogs) in a marine protein matrix, generating defined degradation products that directly correlate with the development of negative sensory attributes and reduced antioxidant capacity.
proposals.method: Marine protein hydrolysates will be fortified with phenolic extracts from underutilized fruits (e.g., Jerivá, Butiá) at varying levels. Samples will undergo controlled dynamic temperature cycles simulating supply chain conditions. Degradation kinetics will be monitored via UPLC-MS/MS to identify specific phenolic breakdown compounds. Sensory analysis (e.g., bitterness, off-flavor) and chemical assays (ORAC, protein-phenolic interaction studies) will be conducted at intervals. Data will be modeled using non-linear regression and response surface methodology to build a predictive time-temperature-fortification matrix.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic understanding of how dynamic temperature stress drives phenolic degradation and off-flavor formation in fortified protein systems. The resulting predictive matrix will offer food scientists a practical tool to optimize fortification levels and recommend stabilization strategies, thereby maximizing the shelf-life quality and health benefits of functional food products.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond static storage models to investigate the complex, non-linear interactions in a dynamic system, explicitly linking specific phenolic degradation pathways catalyzed by temperature fluctuations to measurable losses in sensory and nutritional quality within a relevant marine protein matrix.

proposals.title: Risk-Prioritized Toxicological Profiling of Phenolic Degradants from Marine and Tropical Fruit Byproducts Under Simulated Food Processing Stress
proposals.motivation: The nutraceutical and food industries increasingly valorize phenolic-rich marine and plant byproducts, but the toxicological fate of these bioactive compounds under harsh processing (e.g., high heat, pH extremes) is poorly understood. Pre-2026 research, including our work on tropical fruits and marine hydrolysates, focuses on beneficial properties, creating a critical knowledge gap regarding potential processing-induced toxicants. A systematic framework to identify and prioritize hazardous degradants is essential for ensuring the safety of novel food ingredients and nutraceuticals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subjecting phenolic extracts from marine and underutilized fruit byproducts to defined, extreme processing stresses will generate a library of degradants, among which specific, high-risk compounds can be prioritized through integrated computational and high-throughput screening, revealing novel toxicological mechanisms.
proposals.method: Phenolic-rich extracts from marine protein hydrolysates and tropical fruit byproducts (e.g., Jerivá, Butiá) will be subjected to controlled thermal, alkaline, and oxidative stresses to generate degradant libraries, characterized via UPLC-MS/MS. In silico toxicity prediction will be performed using the OECD QSAR Toolbox and Toxtree for initial prioritization. High-priority compounds will then be screened using high-throughput in vitro assays (e.g., Ames fluctuation, HepG2 cytotoxicity, and ROS generation). The most potent degradants will undergo targeted toxicological assessment, including mechanistic studies on pathways like Nrf2/ARE and MAPK signaling.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, closed-loop decision-making framework linking specific food processing parameters to the generation of toxicologically relevant phenolic degradants. It will provide the first risk-prioritized toxicological profile for degradants from marine and tropical fruit phenolics, directly informing safer processing protocols for the nutraceutical industry.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel by applying an adversarial, hazard-identification lens to the study of phenolic-rich food matrices, shifting focus from antioxidant benefits to proactive toxicological risk assessment of processing-induced chemical transformations, which is largely absent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Circular Biorefinery Approach: Integrated Extraction, Stabilization, and Formulation of Phenolic-Rich Protein Hydrolysates from Marine and Agri-Food Byproducts
proposals.motivation: Agri-food and marine processing residues are rich sources of phenolic compounds and proteins, but their valorization is hindered by the instability of extracted phenolics and the lack of integrated processes that deliver standardized, application-ready ingredients. Current research, including our work on underutilized fruits and marine hydrolysates, often stops at extraction and basic characterization, failing to bridge the gap to commercial viability due to ingredient degradation and variable bioactivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coupling targeted green extraction (e.g., subcritical water, ethanol-water) of phenolics from protein-rich residues like fish processing byproducts or fruit pomace with immediate in-line encapsulation onto food-grade protein carriers (e.g., whey or pea protein isolates) will yield stabilized, synergistic phenolic-protein ingredient modules with standardized antioxidant capacity and enhanced functionality for food and nutraceutical applications.
proposals.method: Selected byproducts (e.g., jerivá pomace, fish frame hydrolysates) will undergo sequential green extraction using a Design of Experiments (DoE)-optimized tunable solvent system (varying pH, temperature, ethanol/water ratio) to co-extract phenolics and peptides. The crude extract will be directly fed into an in-line encapsulation unit employing spray-drying or electrostatic complexation with carrier proteins. Process parameters will be continuously evaluated using a real-time techno-economic assessment (TEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) model built in Python/SimaPro, optimizing for cost, environmental impact, and final ingredient specifications (e.g., phenolic content, antioxidant activity via ORAC/DPPH, powder solubility).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a proven, integrated biorefinery platform that transforms variable biomass into stabilized, value-added ingredients, moving beyond lab-scale extraction. It will provide a novel methodology for the simultaneous stabilization and functionalization of phenolic extracts, creating synergistic antioxidant-protein complexes. The real-time TEA/LCA model will offer a critical decision-support tool for scalable, economically viable biorefinery design.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, process-integrated coupling of extraction with immediate stabilization/formulation, preventing phenolic degradation, and the use of a real-time techno-economic-environmental feedback loop to dynamically guide process optimization for specific end-use specifications, which is not a standard practice in the field.

proposals.title: Microbial Bioleaching-Inspired Pre-treatment for Enhanced Recovery of Phenolic Antioxidants from Marine and Fruit Processing Hydrolysates
proposals.motivation: The food industry generates vast quantities of lignocellulosic by-products (e.g., fruit pomace, marine shells) that are rich in bound phenolic antioxidants but are underutilized due to inefficient extraction. Conventional solvent-based methods are often energy-intensive, use harsh chemicals, and yield suboptimal phenolic recovery due to the recalcitrant matrix. An environmentally benign, selective, and efficient pre-treatment method is needed to valorize these residues into high-value nutraceutical ingredients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tailored microbial consortium, analogous to those used in biomining for metal solubilization, can selectively degrade the structural polysaccharides and lignin in food residues, thereby liberating bound phenolic compounds and significantly enhancing their extractability and antioxidant potency.
proposals.method: We will screen and engineer microbial consortia (e.g., fungi like *Aspergillus* spp. and bacteria like *Bacillus* spp.) known for producing lignocellulolytic enzymes (laccases, peroxidases, cellulases). These consortia will be applied to pre-treat defined food residues (e.g., jerivá pomace, shrimp shell hydrolysates). The bioleaching process parameters (pH, temperature, duration, microbial strain ratio) will be optimized using a Response Surface Methodology. The efficacy will be evaluated by measuring the yield of target phenolics (via UPLC-MS/MS), antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH assays), and the resulting protein-phenolic interactions in the final extracts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, green extraction paradigm for food bioactives, directly translating a core principle from industrial biomining. It will provide a scalable method to increase the commercial viability of food by-products, generating standardized, antioxidant-rich phenolic extracts with potential applications in functional foods and nutraceuticals.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and methodological analogy from metallurgical bioleaching to phenolic extraction, moving beyond simple enzymatic hydrolysis to the use of engineered, synergistic microbial consortia for targeted matrix deconstruction in food systems.

proposals.title: Interfacial Rheology-Guided Extraction of Phenolic-Rich Marine Protein Hydrolysates for the Rational Design of Multifunctional Clean-Label Emulsifiers
proposals.motivation: The food industry urgently needs sustainable, clean-label emulsifiers that also deliver antioxidant functionality. Valuable phenolic compounds and proteins with surface-active potential are abundant in underutilized marine by-products and fruit processing waste. Current extraction methods optimize for yield or total phenolic content, not for the specific interfacial material properties required for emulsion stabilization, leading to unpredictable performance in complex food matrices.
proposals.hypothesis: That targeted extraction parameters (e.g., pH, enzyme type, solvent) can be rationally optimized using interfacial rheological feedback to isolate phenolic-protein complexes from food by-products, yielding fractions with superior, predictable emulsifying and antioxidant properties compared to conventionally extracted materials.
proposals.method: Marine processing by-products (e.g., shrimp shells, fish frames) will be subjected to enzymatic hydrolysis under varied conditions (pH, temperature, enzyme type) to generate protein hydrolysates. Concurrently, phenolic-rich extracts will be obtained from fruit by-products like jerivá and butiá pomace using solvent extraction. These fractions will be combined and their interfacial properties (dilatational elasticity, viscosity via pendant drop/bubble tensiometry) measured. A feedback loop will use these rheological metrics to iteratively refine the extraction and blending protocols, aiming to maximize interfacial film strength. The final optimized complexes will be tested in model oil-in-water emulsions for stability and antioxidant capacity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, functionality-driven extraction paradigm that directly links process parameters to end-use performance in emulsions. It will contribute new, high-value applications for food by-products by creating dual-purpose (emulsifier + antioxidant) ingredients. The methodology provides a data-driven framework for the rational design of clean-label ingredients with predictable behavior.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using interfacial rheology as a real-time diagnostic tool to guide the extraction process itself, rather than merely as a final characterization step. This actively engineers functionality by creating a direct feedback loop between soft matter physics and food chemistry processing.

proposals.title: Quantitative PK-PD Modeling of Marine Protein Hydrolysate Phenolics: Linking Metabolite Kinetics to Dynamic Antioxidant and Anti-inflammatory Biomarker Responses in Humans
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive pre-2026 evidence for the antioxidant potential of phenolic compounds from marine and fruit byproducts, their in vivo bioactivity remains poorly quantified. Current human studies rely on correlative, single-time-point measurements, failing to establish causal, time-dependent relationships between specific circulating metabolites and biological effects. This gap limits the development of these compounds as validated nutraceuticals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying an integrated pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK-PD) modeling framework to human intervention studies will reveal that the biological effects of dietary phenolics are directly mediated by specific phase-II metabolites, with quantifiable delays and effect magnitudes predictable from their plasma concentration-time profiles.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled human intervention study with a characterized phenolic-rich extract from a marine protein hydrolysate. Serial blood samples will be collected over 24h post-consumption. Pharmacokinetic (PK) data will be generated via UPLC-MS/MS quantification of parent phenolics and their sulfate/glucuronide metabolites. High-frequency pharmacodynamic (PD) data will capture dynamic changes in plasma antioxidant capacity (ORAC, FRAP), protein carbonyls, and key inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α). Data will be fitted using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM) to build mechanistic PK-PD models (e.g., indirect response or transit compartment models) linking metabolite concentrations to biomarker time courses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, mechanistic models predicting the in vivo antioxidant and anti-inflammatory bioactivity of marine-derived phenolics based on their metabolism. It will establish a rigorous analytical framework to move from association to causation in nutritional bioactivity research, directly supporting the nutraceutical potential of underutilized marine resources.
proposals.novelty: While PK-PD modeling is standard in pharmacology, its application to dynamically model the bioactivity of dietary phenolics and their metabolites in humans is novel. This approach transcends the standard 'dose-response at a single endpoint' paradigm dominant in pre-2026 nutritional science.

proposals.title: Development of a High-Throughput Screening Platform for the Application-Specific Selection of Antioxidants in Complex Food Matrices
proposals.motivation: Selecting the optimal antioxidant for a specific food application remains a major challenge due to the complex interplay between antioxidant efficacy, stability, and matrix interactions. Current research, including our work on marine hydrolysates and tropical fruit byproducts, relies on low-throughput, pairwise comparisons that are inefficient and fail to capture real-world performance. A systematic, data-driven approach is urgently needed to rationally select antioxidants for applications like functional foods, emulsions, and acidic beverages, maximizing their nutraceutical potential and shelf-life.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting high-throughput screening (HTS) methodologies from pharmaceutical discovery will enable the rapid generation of multi-parameter performance profiles for antioxidants, revealing clear, application-specific efficacy and stability rankings that are not apparent from single-assay studies.
proposals.method: We will develop an automated platform using robotic liquid handlers to prepare libraries of phenolic compounds (e.g., from marine, fruit, and plant sources) and synthetic alternatives in model food matrices (oil-in-water emulsions, buffered acidic systems, protein-rich solutions). Each sample will be subjected in parallel to a battery of assays: chemical (ORAC, DPPH, FRAP) and cellular (CAA-2,2'-azobis(2-amidinopropane) dihydrochloride (ABAP)-induced oxidation in HepG2 cells). Stability will be monitored in real-time under controlled temperature and light, with degradation kinetics tracked via UPLC-MS/MS. Data will be integrated using multivariate analysis (principal component analysis) to create performance heatmaps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, comparative database linking antioxidant chemical structure to multi-faceted performance in relevant food systems. It will deliver a novel methodological framework for the food industry to make evidence-based, cost-effective decisions on antioxidant selection. The platform will also accelerate the valorization of underutilized natural sources, like marine byproducts and fruit waste, by efficiently identifying their most promising bioactive fractions.
proposals.novelty: While HTS is standard in drug discovery, its systematic adaptation for comparative antioxidant performance in complex, variable food matrices is novel. This moves beyond static chemical assays to a dynamic, application-oriented screening paradigm that integrates matrix effects, cellular activity, and stability kinetics in a single automated workflow.

proposals.title: A Predator-Prey Pharmacodynamic Model to Predict the In Vivo Antioxidant Efficacy of Marine Protein Hydrolysate-Derived Phenolic Compounds
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive in vitro characterization of phenolic antioxidants from marine and fruit byproducts, predicting their in vivo dose-response remains a major challenge. Static models fail to capture the dynamic, non-linear interplay between absorbed antioxidants and endogenously generated reactive oxygen species (ROS), leading to unreliable efficacy predictions and potential for pro-oxidant effects at high doses. A mechanistic framework is needed to translate in vitro antioxidant capacity into meaningful in vivo outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: The time-course of in vivo antioxidant activity for a specific phenolic compound (e.g., gallic acid or pterostilbene) can be accurately modeled by adapting the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey equations, where the phenolic acts as the 'predator' and ROS as the 'prey', integrated with pharmacokinetic data on compound absorption and distribution.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the pharmacokinetics (PK) of a model phenolic (e.g., from jerivá or marine protein hydrolysates) in a rodent model via plasma sampling and UPLC-MS/MS analysis. This PK data will feed into a pharmacodynamic (PD) model built on modified Lotka-Volterra equations. Model parameters (ROS generation rate, phenolic-ROS reaction/scavenging rate, phenolic decay rate) will be estimated by fitting the model to time-course data on plasma oxidative status markers (e.g., ORAC, lipid peroxides) following phenolic dosing. Model validation will involve testing predictions against a separate dose-response study.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systems-based computational tool to predict the optimal dosing windows and potential efficacy ceilings for dietary antioxidants. It will advance the field of nutraceutical science from static chemical assays to dynamic, physiologically relevant modeling. The framework can be applied to optimize the delivery of antioxidant-rich fractions from underutilized marine and plant sources.
proposals.novelty: This is the first application of ecological population dynamics models to quantitatively describe the in vivo action of dietary antioxidants. It moves beyond correlation-based approaches to a mechanistic, kinetic model of the direct scavenging interaction within a biological compartment.

proposals.title: Predicting In Vivo Antioxidant Efficacy of Marine Protein Hydrolysate Phenolics: A Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive in vitro evidence for the antioxidant potential of phenolic compounds from marine food hydrolysates and underutilized fruits, translating these findings to effective human doses remains a major challenge. Current nutraceutical development lacks the rigorous, predictive frameworks standard in pharmaceutical discovery, leading to inefficient human trials and uncertain bioactivity. This gap hinders the commercialization of promising bioactive fractions identified in pre-2026 research on marine and tropical fruit byproducts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a mechanism-based pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) model, parameterized with in vitro and preclinical data, can accurately predict the in vivo dose-response relationship for the antioxidant effects of phenolic-rich fractions from marine protein hydrolysates.
proposals.method: Phenolic-rich fractions will be isolated from defined marine protein hydrolysates (e.g., from shrimp or fish processing byproducts) and characterized via UPLC-MS/MS for compounds like gallic acid and other phenolics. A multi-compartment pharmacokinetic (PK) model will be developed using absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME) data from rodent studies. This PK model will be linked to a pharmacodynamic (PD) model (e.g., an indirect response or Emax model) where the driving force is the plasma concentration of key phenolics, and the measured response is a validated in vivo biomarker of oxidative stress (e.g., plasma ORAC or specific lipid peroxidation products). Model fitting and simulation will be performed using specialized software (e.g., Phoenix WinNonlin or NONMEM).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative tool to bridge the gap between in vitro antioxidant assays and in vivo efficacy for food-derived bioactives. It will establish a methodology for rationally determining effective human doses of nutraceutical fractions, thereby de-risking and accelerating their development. The project will deliver a validated PK/PD model specific to marine phenolic antioxidants, a significant advancement in food science and nutraceutical research.
proposals.novelty: While PK/PD modeling is standard in drug development, its application to predict the dose-response of antioxidant phenolics from food matrices, particularly marine protein hydrolysates, is novel and non-obvious. This approach moves beyond correlative in vitro studies to a predictive, mechanistic framework for food bioactives.

proposals.title: pH- and Enzyme-Responsive Zein-Alginate-Chitosan Nanocapsules for Targeted Intestinal Delivery of Jerivá and Butiá Phenolic Antioxidants
proposals.motivation: Phenolic antioxidants from underutilized tropical fruits like Jerivá and Butiá show significant nutraceutical potential, but their health impact is limited by poor stability during food processing and low bioavailability due to degradation in the upper GI tract. Current encapsulation methods primarily focus on protection, lacking precise control over release location, which is critical for optimal absorption and bioactivity. A stimuli-responsive delivery system is needed to maximize the functional benefits of these valuable marine and plant bioactives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that core-shell nanocapsules, fabricated with a zein core and alginate-chitosan shell, will protect phenolic antioxidants during thermal processing and selectively release them in the intestinal environment via pH-triggered shell dissolution and protease (pepsin, pancreatin) action, leading to significantly enhanced in vitro bioavailability and antioxidant activity compared to non-encapsulated or conventionally encapsulated phenolics.
proposals.method: Phenolic-rich extracts from Jerivá and Butiá byproducts will be encapsulated using a sequential ionic gelation and complex coacervation method. A core of zein and phenolics will be formed, then coated with alternating layers of alginate (anionic) and chitosan (cationic) to create a pH-responsive shell. Nanocapsule characterization will include size (DLS), morphology (TEM), encapsulation efficiency (UPLC-MS/MS), and stability under simulated food processing (heat, shear). Release kinetics and location will be validated using a standardized in vitro gastrointestinal digestion model (INFOGEST), measuring phenolic release at gastric (pH 2.0, pepsin) and intestinal (pH 7.0, pancreatin, bile salts) phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, food-grade nano-delivery platform for the targeted intestinal delivery of sensitive bioactives, directly enhancing their nutraceutical application. It will deliver specific data on the bioavailability and retained antioxidant capacity of encapsulated tropical fruit phenolics, contributing to the valorization of underutilized byproducts. The research will establish a methodology for designing stimuli-responsive systems that move beyond passive protection to active release control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the application of a multi-layered, food polymer-based core-shell system specifically engineered to respond to the sequential pH and enzymatic stimuli of the GI tract for targeted intestinal delivery, applied to the under-explored phenolic profiles of Jerivá and Butiá. This integrates Shahidi's expertise in marine/plant phenolics and protein hydrolysates with advanced, yet pre-2026, nano-encapsulation design principles.

proposals.title: Enhancing the Stability and Bioactivity of Marine Protein Hydrolysate-Phenolic Complexes Using Zein-Chitosan Core-Shell Nanoencapsulation
proposals.motivation: Phenolic antioxidants from marine and underutilized plant sources, such as those studied in Dr. Shahidi's recent work, are highly susceptible to degradation during food processing and storage, limiting their nutraceutical application. While protein-phenolic complexes can enhance stability, they often lack sufficient protection against harsh thermal and pH conditions. Advanced encapsulation systems from materials science offer a promising solution to this stability challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that encapsulating marine protein hydrolysate-phenolic complexes within a zein-chitosan core-shell nanoparticle system will significantly improve their thermal and oxidative stability during simulated food processing, thereby preserving their antioxidant and bioactive properties more effectively than non-encapsulated complexes.
proposals.method: Marine protein hydrolysates (e.g., from shrimp or fish byproducts) will be complexed with phenolic extracts from Jerivá or Butiá fruit byproducts. These complexes will be encapsulated using a two-step method: first, forming a core via anti-solvent precipitation of zein, and second, applying an electrostatic deposition coating of chitosan. Nanoparticle characteristics (size, zeta potential, encapsulation efficiency) will be analyzed via dynamic light scattering and HPLC. Stability will be tested under accelerated conditions (heat, UV light, varying pH), with degradation monitored by UPLC-MS/MS for phenolic content and in vitro assays (ORAC, DPPH) for antioxidant capacity retention.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, food-grade delivery system specifically designed for protein-phenolic complexes, directly applicable to functional food development. It will deliver quantitative data on the protective efficacy of core-shell nanoparticles against key processing stressors, bridging a gap between materials science encapsulation techniques and practical food biochemistry.
proposals.novelty: While protein-phenolic interactions and encapsulation are known separately, the targeted application of a zein-chitosan core-shell system to stabilize pre-formed marine protein hydrolysate-phenolic complexes for processing resilience is novel. This approach synergistically combines the bioactive carrier (hydrolysate) with a dedicated protective shell.

proposals.title: Chemometric Fingerprinting and Metabolic Flux Modeling for Efficacy-Based Standardization of Marine Protein Hydrolysate Antioxidants
proposals.motivation: Current standardization of natural antioxidant extracts, such as those from marine protein hydrolysates and underutilized fruit byproducts, relies on quantifying single marker compounds (e.g., gallic acid) or total phenolic content. This approach fails to predict the complex, synergistic biological activity of the extract, leading to variable efficacy in food and nutraceutical applications. A new paradigm is needed to link comprehensive chemical profiles directly to quantifiable impacts on cellular metabolic pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chemometric analysis of detailed UPLC-MS/MS chemical fingerprints can be integrated with constraint-based metabolic flux analysis to predict and standardize the specific effects of complex natural extracts on core cellular antioxidant and redox pathways.
proposals.method: Marine protein hydrolysates and fruit byproduct extracts will be chemically profiled using UPLC-MS/MS. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA) will identify key metabolite patterns correlated with in vitro antioxidant capacity. These chemometric patterns will serve as quantitative input constraints for a genome-scale metabolic model (e.g., Recon3D) to simulate flux distributions in the glutathione and NADPH metabolism pathways, predicting the extract's impact on cellular redox homeostasis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, efficacy-driven framework for standardizing bioactive natural extracts, moving beyond chemical markers to functional metabolic impact. It will provide a predictive tool for identifying optimal extraction parameters and sources for consistent antioxidant activity, directly applicable to the nutraceutical and functional food industries.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of chemometric pattern recognition with genome-scale metabolic modeling to create bioactivity-based standards is a novel integration of food chemistry and systems biology. This approach is distinct from traditional single-marker standardization or purely correlative chemometric models.

proposals.title: Metabolic Flux Analysis of Cellular Redox Networks: Quantifying the Impact of Marine Protein Hydrolysate-Derived Phenolic Compounds on Antioxidant Pathway Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Dietary phenolics from marine and plant sources are known for their antioxidant potential, but their precise, quantitative impact on intracellular metabolic pathways remains poorly mapped. A systems-level understanding is needed to move beyond static concentration measurements and predict how these bioactive compounds modulate cellular redox homeostasis and resilience to oxidative stress, which is central to their nutraceutical and chemopreventive value.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific phenolic compounds identified in marine protein hydrolysates and underutilized fruit byproducts will induce distinct, quantifiable shifts in central carbon and antioxidant metabolic fluxes, particularly through the pentose phosphate pathway and glutathione synthesis, thereby enhancing the cellular redox buffering capacity.
proposals.method: We will apply 13C isotopic tracer analysis (using U-13C-glucose) coupled with LC-MS/MS metabolomics to HepG2 cells treated with phenolic fractions from marine hydrolysates (e.g., from shrimp or fish processing byproducts) and Jerivá/Butiá fruit extracts. Metabolic flux analysis (MFA) will be performed using established constraint-based modeling software (e.g., COBRA Toolbox in MATLAB) to compute flux distributions through core metabolic networks, including glycolysis, TCA cycle, pentose phosphate pathway, and glutathione metabolism. Flux differences between treated and control cells will be statistically analyzed to identify key redirected nodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative flux maps linking specific dietary phenolic profiles to dynamic changes in antioxidant metabolism. It will establish a mechanistic, systems-based framework for evaluating the bioactivity of nutraceutical compounds, moving from correlative antioxidant assays to predictive metabolic engineering models for optimal health benefit formulation.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Shahidi's prior work identifies and characterizes phenolic compounds, applying metabolic flux analysis from systems biology to quantify their real-time effect on metabolic pathways represents a novel, dynamic integration of food chemistry and computational systems biology not previously applied to these marine and fruit-derived bioactives.

proposals.title: Chemometric-Driven Valorization of Thyme Distillation Waste: Optimizing Extraction for Standardized, Authenticatable Antioxidant Hydrolysates
proposals.motivation: The essential oil industry generates substantial deodorized herb waste, a potential source of valuable phenolic antioxidants, yet its valorization is inefficient and inconsistent. Current extraction optimization focuses solely on maximizing yield, ignoring the complex, variable chemical profile of the waste, which leads to non-standardized products unsuitable for high-value nutraceutical applications. There is a critical need for intelligent, data-driven methods to transform this by-product into a reliable, quality-controlled source of bioactive compounds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chemometric analysis of initial thyme waste spectral fingerprints can identify key source-specific variables that correlate with optimal antioxidant yield and a characteristic phenolic profile. This will allow for the development of a guided extraction protocol that targets a reproducible, authenticable chemical signature rather than just total phenolics.
proposals.method: Deodorized thyme waste will be characterized using FTIR and UPLC-MS/MS to build a spectral/chromatographic database. Chemometric techniques, specifically Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA), will correlate this fingerprint data with antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH) and phenolic acid profiles from various solvent extracts (e.g., aqueous, ethanolic) and enzymatic hydrolysates. The resulting models will guide the selection of solvent systems and hydrolysis conditions to optimize for the targeted signature. Final protocol validation will include authentication testing via the developed PLS-DA model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, chemometrics-guided framework for converting variable agricultural waste into a standardized nutraceutical ingredient. It will provide a validated extraction protocol for thyme waste antioxidants and establish a proof-of-concept for using source fingerprinting for both process optimization and ongoing quality control and origin authentication in the food and supplement industries.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of chemometric pattern recognition at the feedstock stage to directly steer extraction parameters for a target chemical signature is a novel, proactive approach to by-product valorization. This moves beyond reactive optimization to create a traceable, 'designer' extract with built-in authentication, a significant advance over conventional methods.

proposals.title: Chemometric Fingerprinting of Marine Protein Hydrolysates and Underutilized Fruit Byproducts for Authentication and Standardization of Antioxidant Extracts
proposals.motivation: The global nutraceutical and functional food market demands reliable, standardized natural antioxidant ingredients. However, variations in source material (e.g., marine species, fruit cultivars, harvest conditions) and extraction parameters lead to inconsistent bioactive profiles, undermining product efficacy and authenticity. Robust, data-driven methods are needed to authenticate sources and ensure batch-to-batch consistency of antioxidant-rich extracts like protein hydrolysates and phenolic fractions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate chemometric analysis of comprehensive chromatographic (UPLC-MS/MS) and spectroscopic data can generate unique chemical fingerprints to accurately authenticate the botanical or marine source of antioxidant extracts and discriminate between extracts processed under different conditions.
proposals.method: Antioxidant-rich extracts will be prepared from defined sources (e.g., specific marine hydrolysates, Jerivá and Butiá byproducts) under controlled extraction parameters. Their phenolic and peptide profiles will be characterized via UPLC-MS/MS and FTIR. The multivariate data will be analyzed using principal component analysis (PCA) for exploratory pattern recognition and partial least squares-discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) or soft independent modeling of class analogies (SIMCA) to build classification models for source authentication and process standardization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated chemometric framework for the authentication and quality control of natural antioxidant extracts, directly supporting the standardization of nutraceutical ingredients. It will demonstrate the specific chemical markers responsible for class discrimination, linking source identity and processing history to bioactivity potential.
proposals.novelty: While chemometrics is used in food authentication, its targeted application for standardizing the antioxidant potential of underutilized tropical fruit byproducts and marine protein hydrolysates—integrating specific phenolic and peptide profiling data—is novel and addresses a critical gap in valorizing these sustainable resources.

proposals.title: Real-Time, Single-Cell Quantification of Antioxidant Response to Marine Protein Hydrolysate-Derived Phenolics Using a Genetically Encoded Rationetric Biosensor
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Shahidi and others has extensively characterized the phenolic profiles and bulk antioxidant capacity of marine hydrolysates and underutilized fruit byproducts. However, the dynamic, cell-specific antioxidant effects of these compounds in a living system remain unknown, limiting our understanding of their true bioactivity and potential nutraceutical efficacy against oxidative stress-related pathologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific phenolic compounds identified in marine protein hydrolysates (e.g., gallic acid derivatives) and tropical fruit byproducts will elicit rapid, dose-dependent reductions in intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) in single living cells, and that this response will vary significantly between cell types.
proposals.method: We will employ a genetically encoded, rationetric biosensor, HyPer7, expressed in human renal (RCC) and hepatic (HepG2) cell lines. Single cells will be imaged via live-cell confocal microscopy in microfluidic chambers. Following induction of oxidative stress with menadione or sunitinib, cells will be perfused with purified phenolics (e.g., pterostilbene, gallic acid) or crude hydrolysate fractions. The HyPer7 fluorescence ratio (ex 488 nm / ex 405 nm) will be tracked in real-time to quantify spatiotemporal changes in H2O2, a key ROS. Data analysis will use custom Python scripts for single-cell tracking and kinetic modeling of antioxidant response curves.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, kinetic evidence of the intracellular antioxidant potency of specific marine and fruit-derived phenolics at single-cell resolution. It will bridge the gap between chemical antioxidant assays and biological activity, offering a robust platform to screen and validate nutraceutical candidates for targeted oxidative stress mitigation.
proposals.novelty: While Shahidi's prior work identifies compounds and measures bulk activity, this proposal innovates by applying a cutting-edge, pre-2026 biosensor technology (HyPer7) to measure real-time, subcellular antioxidant kinetics in living cells, moving from food chemistry to functional cell biology.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Marine Protein-Phenolic Conjugates as Targeted Antioxidant Delivery Systems: A Pharmaceutical Formulation Approach
proposals.motivation: Phenolic antioxidants from marine and plant sources, such as gallic acid, pterostilbene, and 6-shogaol, exhibit significant health-promoting potential but suffer from poor stability, low bioavailability, and non-targeted release in the gastrointestinal tract. This limits their efficacy as nutraceuticals. Applying the established principles of pharmaceutical drug delivery, where material design is dictated by the active ingredient's target site and release profile, offers a transformative framework to engineer food matrices for precision phenolic delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating specific phenolic compounds as 'active pharmaceutical ingredients' and using food-grade marine protein hydrolysates and biopolymers as functional excipients, we can rationally design composite food matrices (e.g., emulsion-filled gels, coated particles) that program the stability, intestinal site-specific release, and enhanced antioxidant bioavailability of the encapsulated phenolics.
proposals.method: The research will employ an inverse design workflow: (1) Characterize target phenolics (e.g., from palo prieto, jerivá) for their degradation kinetics and intended site of action. (2) Use marine protein (e.g., from fish processing byproducts) hydrolysates and other biopolymers to create core-shell microcapsules and emulsion-filled gels via controlled gelation and layer-by-layer assembly. (3) Systematically test the release profiles of phenolics from these matrices using in vitro gastrointestinal digestion models (INFOGEST protocol) coupled with UPLC-MS/MS quantification and antioxidant capacity assays (ORAC, cellular).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, principle-driven methodology for designing functional foods as targeted delivery systems, moving beyond empirical encapsulation. It will generate fundamental knowledge on structure-release-activity relationships for phenolic-protein matrices and valorize underutilized marine and tropical fruit byproducts as high-value nutraceutical ingredients.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct analogical transfer of the pharmaceutical 'inverse design' paradigm to food structuring, explicitly programming food matrices for targeted phenolic release based on the compound's mechanism of action, rather than merely improving general stability or masking bitterness.

proposals.title: Systematic Discovery of Novel Antioxidant Peptides and Phenolic Compounds from Marine and Agri-food Processing Hydrolysates via Bioactivity-Guided Fractionation and UPLC-MS/MS
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the valorization of food processing by-products (e.g., marine discards, fruit pomace) primarily focuses on recovering known compounds or bulk applications. This approach overlooks their potential as unique sources of novel bioactive structures. Given the rising demand for natural antioxidants in food and nutraceutical sectors, a paradigm shift is needed to treat these discards as primary discovery matrices rather than secondary waste streams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that enzymatic hydrolysates of underutilized marine and tropical fruit processing by-products contain unique, structurally novel antioxidant compounds (peptides and phenolics) that are not present or are minor components in the original raw materials.
proposals.method: By-products (e.g., fish protein hydrolysates, jerivá/butiá seed cake) will be subjected to sequential enzymatic hydrolysis using food-grade proteases (Alcalase, Flavourzyme) and carbohydrases. Bioactivity-guided fractionation will be performed using membrane ultrafiltration and semi-preparative HPLC based on real-time antioxidant assays (ORAC, DPPH, cellular ROS). Active fractions will be analyzed via UPLC-ESI-QTOF-MS/MS for de novo identification of novel peptide sequences and phenolic conjugates, supported by molecular networking and in silico docking to predict structure-activity relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a targeted methodology for discovering novel antioxidants from waste, moving beyond mere quantification of known entities. It will provide a detailed phenolic and peptide profile of specific underutilized by-products, identifying new lead compounds with potential for renal cancer cell model testing, as per the team's recent work on RLIP76 pathways.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard valorization studies that quantify known compounds, this proposal treats processing discards as primary discovery sources, employing bioactivity-guided fractionation coupled with advanced MS/MS to specifically target and identify novel antioxidant structures generated or concentrated during processing.

proposals.title: Techno-Economic and Life Cycle Assessment Modeling for Valorizing Marine Protein Hydrolysates and Phenolic-Rich Fruit Byproducts into Functional Food Ingredients
proposals.motivation: The food processing industry generates vast quantities of nutrient-rich discards, such as marine hydrolysates and fruit pomaces, which represent both an environmental burden and a lost economic opportunity. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Shahidi's work, has established the potent antioxidant and bioactive potential of these materials, but their integration into a viable circular supply chain remains unmodeled. A systematic framework is needed to guide industry adoption by quantifying the economic feasibility and environmental benefits of valorizing these streams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating techno-economic analysis (TEA) with life cycle assessment (LCA) will identify optimal, region-specific processing pathways that transform antioxidant-rich marine and fruit processing wastes into high-value functional food ingredients, demonstrating both profitability and reduced environmental impact compared to conventional disposal.
proposals.method: The method involves three concrete steps. First, bioactive compounds (phenolic acids, peptides) will be characterized from selected marine hydrolysates and tropical fruit byproducts (e.g., Butiá) using UPLC-MS/MS and in vitro antioxidant assays. Second, process flow diagrams for scalable extraction and stabilization will be developed. Third, these technical data will feed into a combined TEA/LCA model built using algorithms like Monte Carlo simulation for economic risk analysis and the ReCiPe 2016 midpoint impact assessment method for environmental footprinting, comparing scenarios from waste to functional ingredient.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative decision-support model for the food industry to implement circular economy practices, moving beyond lab-scale proof-of-concept. It will deliver specific, data-driven pathways for creating new revenue streams from waste while reducing the carbon and waste footprint of food processing, contributing directly to sustainable development goals.
proposals.novelty: While individual studies on bioactivity exist, the novelty lies in the integrated application of rigorous TEA and LCA modeling specifically to the co-valorization of marine and fruit waste streams for functional foods, creating a transferable framework that links chemical potential directly to economic and environmental outcomes.

proposals.title: Cascading Biorefinery Fractionation of Marine and Tropical Fruit By-Products: Integrated Recovery of Phenolic Antioxidants and Protein Hydrolysates
proposals.motivation: The valorization of food processing by-products is a critical challenge for sustainable food systems.
