Marine and tropical fruit wastes are rich in bioactive phenolic compounds and proteins, but conventional extraction methods often target single components, leading to inefficient resource use and residual waste. Adapting the cascading biorefinery principles from biofuel production offers a holistic solution to maximize value recovery from these complex matrices.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying sequential, cascading extraction techniques (e.g., aqueous, enzymatic, solvent-based) to marine (e.g., shrimp shells, fish frames) and tropical fruit (e.g., jerivá pomace) by-products will efficiently fractionate distinct, high-value streams—specifically, phenolic-rich antioxidant extracts and protein hydrolysates—while minimizing waste generation.
proposals.method: The approach will employ a sequential fractionation protocol. First, mild aqueous or green solvent (e.g., ethanol-water) extraction will recover free and loosely bound phenolic antioxidants, monitored via UPLC-MS/MS for compounds like gallic acid and pterostilbene. The resultant solid residue will then undergo targeted enzymatic hydrolysis (using proteases like Alcalase or Flavourzyme) to generate protein hydrolysates, with antioxidant capacity (e.g., ORAC, DPPH) and peptide profiles analyzed. Finally, advanced techniques like membrane filtration or adsorption chromatography may be applied to further purify fractions from the hydrolysates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, integrated biorefinery blueprint for the co-production of nutraceutical antioxidants and bioactive peptides from underutilized food wastes. It will expand the application of Shahidi's expertise in marine and fruit phenolics/proteins into sustainable process engineering, contributing data on the synergistic potential of cascading extractions for enhancing total phenolic yield and protein functionality.
proposals.novelty: While biorefinery concepts are established in biofuel contexts, their systematic application to fractionate both phenolic antioxidants and protein hydrolysates from the same food waste stream in a cascading sequence is novel. This moves beyond single-compound extraction, aligning with circular economy principles for food processing side-streams.

proposals.title: Dose-Dependent Pro-Oxidant Effects of Marine Protein Hydrolysates and Their Phenolic-Enriched Fractions in Model Food Systems
proposals.motivation: The nutraceutical and food industries increasingly fortify products with antioxidant-rich extracts from marine and plant sources. However, pre-2026 research indicates many dietary antioxidants, including phenolic compounds, can exhibit pro-oxidant activity under specific conditions, potentially generating harmful reactive species. This paradoxical effect is poorly characterized for complex food matrices like protein hydrolysates, which are key to Dr. Shahidi's work, creating a critical knowledge gap for safe and effective formulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that marine protein hydrolysates and their phenolic-enriched fractions will exhibit a concentration-dependent transition from antioxidant to pro-oxidant activity in model lipid and protein systems, and that this transition point is influenced by the specific phenolic profile and the presence of transition metal ions.
proposals.method: We will prepare hydrolysates from underutilized marine sources (e.g., shrimp shell, fish frame) and isolate phenolic-enriched fractions. Using a UPLC-MS/MS system, we will characterize their phenolic and gallic acid content. The pro-oxidant threshold will be determined in two model systems: 1) a β-carotene-linoleate emulsion, monitoring co-oxidation, and 2) a bovine serum albumin system, measuring protein carbonyl formation. Dose-response curves (0.1-10 mg/mL) will be generated under both metal-catalyzed (Fe³⁺/ascorbate) and metal-free conditions to identify critical concentration thresholds.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a quantitative framework for the safe dosage of antioxidant-rich marine ingredients in food formulations by defining their pro-oxidant thresholds. It will contribute novel data on the behavior of complex hydrolysate-phenolic mixtures, beyond pure compounds, and offer practical insights for optimizing the bioactivity and stability of functional foods and nutraceuticals.
proposals.novelty: While the pro-oxidant effect of pure phenolics is known, this work is novel in applying Dr. Shahidi's expertise to systematically investigate this inverse problem for complex, food-relevant marine protein hydrolysate-phenolic matrices, linking specific compositional data from UPLC-MS/MS to functional dose-response transitions.

proposals.title: Sequential Valorization of Marine Processing Byproducts: Integrated Recovery of Antioxidant Protein Hydrolysates and Fabrication of Bioactive Composite Films
proposals.motivation: The seafood industry generates significant waste, rich in proteins and phenolic compounds, which is often underutilized. Current valorization strategies typically target a single product stream, such as antioxidant hydrolysates, leaving residual solids with untapped functional potential. An integrated process to extract both high-value antioxidants and create functional structural materials from the same waste stream would dramatically improve economic viability and sustainability in a pre-2026 circular bioeconomy context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential process involving enzymatic hydrolysis of marine byproducts (e.g., shrimp shells, fish frames) will yield protein hydrolysates with potent antioxidant activity, and that the resulting solid residue, when combined with biopolymers like chitosan or gelatin, can form composite films with enhanced mechanical and bioactive properties due to residual phenolic-protein complexes.
proposals.method: The method involves a two-stage process. First, marine byproducts will be subjected to controlled enzymatic hydrolysis (using Alcalase, Flavourzyme) under optimized conditions to produce antioxidant protein hydrolysates; antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH) and phenolic profile (UPLC-MS/MS) will be characterized. Second, the defatted, hydrolyzed solid residue will be incorporated into film-forming solutions of chitosan or gelatin. Films will be cast and evaluated for mechanical strength, water barrier properties, and retained antioxidant/antimicrobial activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, zero-waste blueprint for the complete utilization of marine processing sidestreams. It will contribute specific knowledge on how hydrolysis parameters influence both the extractable antioxidants and the techno-functional properties of the residual matrix for material science applications, advancing the field of integrated biorefining.
proposals.novelty: While Shahidi's prior work has extensively characterized antioxidants from various sources, the novelty lies in the intentional design of a single process chain to co-produce both a nutraceutical extract and a structurally functional material from the same starting biomass, leveraging interactions between residual phenolics and proteins in the post-hydrolysis matrix.

proposals.title: Optimizing Extraction Temperature for Phenolic Diversity and Bioactivity: A Comparative Study of Marine and Plant-Derived Protein Hydrolysates
proposals.motivation: Conventional extraction of phenolic compounds often employs high temperatures (e.g., 75°C) to maximize total phenolic content and antioxidant capacity measured by aggregate assays like Folin-Ciocalteu and DPPH. However, pre-2026 research indicates that heat-sensitive phenolic subclasses, such as certain anthocyanins, flavan-3-ols, and stilbenes (e.g., pterostilbene), can degrade, potentially losing unique bioactivities not captured by standard antioxidant screens. This is critical for valorizing underutilized marine and plant byproducts as functional food ingredients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lower extraction temperatures (e.g., 25-50°C) will better preserve the integrity and diversity of heat-sensitive phenolic subclasses in protein hydrolysates, leading to distinct biological activity profiles (e.g., anti-proliferative, anti-inflammatory) that are not correlated with aggregate antioxidant assays.
proposals.method: Protein hydrolysates will be prepared from marine (e.g., shrimp shell) and plant (e.g., palo prieto, fruit byproducts) sources using enzymatic hydrolysis at controlled temperatures (25°C, 50°C, 75°C). Phenolic profiles will be characterized via UPLC-MS/MS, quantifying specific subclasses (hydroxybenzoic acids, flavanols, stilbenes). Bioactivity will be assessed using standard antioxidant assays (ORAC, FRAP) and cell-based models for anti-proliferative activity (e.g., renal cancer cell lines) and anti-inflammatory markers (e.g., COX-2 inhibition). Statistical analysis (PCA, correlation) will link extraction temperature, phenolic fingerprint, and bioactivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a nuanced framework for phenolic extraction, moving beyond maximizing total yield to optimizing for specific bioactive compound classes. It will deliver targeted protocols for producing protein-phenolic hydrolysates from underutilized sources with tailored biofunctional properties for nutraceutical applications.
proposals.novelty: While temperature effects on total phenolics are known, this study directly challenges the 'one-temperature-fits-all' paradigm by systematically linking sub-75°C extraction to the preservation of distinct phenolic chemistries and their non-antioxidant biological activities in protein hydrolysate matrices.

proposals.title: Chemometric Modeling of Extraction Parameters to Predict Phenolic Fingerprints and Antioxidant Activity in Marine Protein Hydrolysates
proposals.motivation: The extraction of bioactive compounds from marine sources is a key focus in food science, but the relationship between process conditions and the resulting complex phenolic profile is poorly understood. Optimizing for a single compound, like gallic acid, may overlook synergistic effects from the full phenolic fingerprint, limiting the potential to tailor hydrolysates for specific nutraceutical or functional food applications. A systematic, data-driven approach is needed to move beyond trial-and-error optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific combinations of extraction parameters (e.g., solvent, time, temperature, enzyme type) will correlate strongly with distinct phenolic fingerprints in marine protein hydrolysates, and that these fingerprints can be quantitatively linked to antioxidant capacity using chemometric models.
proposals.method: We will produce marine protein hydrolysates using a factorial design varying enzyme (e.g., alcalase, flavourzyme), hydrolysis time, temperature, and solvent polarity. Phenolic profiles will be characterized via UPLC-MS/MS. Chemometric techniques, including Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for pattern recognition and Partial Least Squares Regression (PLSR), will be applied to correlate extraction parameters (X-matrix) with phenolic composition and antioxidant activity (Y-matrix). Key discriminatory compounds will be identified via variable importance in projection (VIP) scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive framework for rationally designing extraction processes to yield marine hydrolysates with targeted phenolic compositions and enhanced bioactivity. It will advance the field from qualitative descriptions to quantitative, model-based optimization, directly supporting the valorization of marine byproducts. The identified key phenolic markers will offer new insights into structure-activity relationships in complex food matrices.
proposals.novelty: While chemometrics is established in metabolomics, its systematic application to link *process parameters* to the detailed phenolic fingerprint of *marine protein hydrolysates* is novel. This approach integrates Shahidi's expertise in marine bioactives and phenolic analysis with process modeling, moving beyond the common practice of reporting composition and activity separately.

proposals.title: Concentration and Purification of Marine Protein Hydrolysate-Derived Phenolic Extracts Using Nanofiltration: Impact on Antioxidant Profile and Bioactivity
proposals.motivation: The aqueous extracts from marine protein hydrolysates are rich in bioactive phenolic compounds with significant antioxidant potential, but conventional concentration methods like evaporation can degrade heat-sensitive phenolics. Membrane separation, widely used in chemical engineering for fractionation, offers a gentle, scalable alternative. Applying this technology to marine bioactives aligns with the growing pre-2026 interest in sustainable, non-thermal processing to preserve nutraceutical value from underutilized sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nanofiltration (NF) membranes with specific molecular weight cut-offs (MWCO) can effectively concentrate and purify phenolic compounds from aqueous extracts of marine protein hydrolysates, resulting in fractions with enhanced antioxidant capacity and distinct phenolic profiles compared to crude extracts.
proposals.method: Aqueous phenolic extracts will be prepared from enzymatically hydrolyzed marine protein sources (e.g., shrimp, fish). These extracts will be processed using a cross-flow nanofiltration system equipped with commercial polymeric NF membranes (e.g., NF270, DK series) with MWCOs of 200-500 Da. Process parameters (transmembrane pressure, cross-flow velocity, pH) will be optimized for flux and phenolic retention. The permeate and retentate fractions will be analyzed for total phenolic content (Folin-Ciocalteu), specific phenolics (UPLC-MS/MS), and antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for applying membrane engineering to valorize marine by-products, providing a method to produce potent, concentrated phenolic ingredients for functional foods. It will deliver critical data on the selectivity of NF membranes for complex phenolic mixtures from marine hydrolysates and correlate processing conditions with bioactivity retention.
proposals.novelty: While membrane filtration is established in food processing (e.g., dairy, juice), its systematic application to concentrate phenolics from the complex aqueous matrix of marine protein hydrolysates is novel. The integration of hydrolysate chemistry with membrane separation engineering to target bioactive preservation is a non-obvious, interdisciplinary advance.

proposals.title: Energy-Efficient Optimization of Phenolic Extraction from Marine Protein Hydrolysates: A Response Surface Methodology Approach to Minimize Energy for a Target Bioactive Yield
proposals.motivation: Conventional extraction of phenolic antioxidants from food matrices, such as marine by-products, often prioritizes maximizing yield without regard to energy consumption, which is economically and environmentally unsustainable. Pre-2026 research by Shahidi and others highlights the nutraceutical potential of marine hydrolysates and phenolic compounds, but lacks frameworks for green process optimization. This work addresses the dual need for bioactive recovery and industrial energy efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying a dual-objective optimization model, we can identify extraction conditions (time, temperature, solvent ratio) that achieve a pre-defined, commercially relevant phenolic yield from a marine protein hydrolysate while minimizing specific energy consumption.
proposals.method: We will employ Response Surface Methodology (RSM) with a Central Composite Design, using solvent concentration, temperature, and time as independent variables. The dual-response optimization will target a minimum phenolic yield (e.g., 50 mg GAE/g) as a constraint while minimizing the calculated energy input (based on heating and agitation). Specific algorithms include the desirability function approach and gradient descent methods within RSM software (e.g., Design-Expert) to solve the constrained minimization problem. Validation will involve UPLC-MS/MS phenolic profiling and antioxidant capacity assays (DPPH, ORAC).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, practical framework for the sustainable valorization of marine by-products, directly contributing to green chemistry in food processing. It will yield a validated, energy-optimized protocol for producing phenolic-rich fractions from marine hydrolysates, enhancing their economic viability as nutraceutical ingredients.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the optimization paradigm from maximizing phenolic yield to minimizing energy input for a target yield, a non-obvious approach in pre-2026 bioactive extraction studies which typically treat energy as a secondary concern.

proposals.title: Marine Protein-Phenolic Co-Hydrolysate Stabilized Nanoemulsions for Enhanced Oxidative Stability and Bioaccessibility of Omega-3 Rich Oils
proposals.motivation: Omega-3 fatty acids are highly susceptible to oxidation, leading to rancidity, loss of nutritional value, and generation of harmful compounds, which limits their application in functional foods. Pre-2026 research by Shahidi and others highlights the potent antioxidant and emulsifying potential of marine-derived protein hydrolysates and phenolic compounds from underutilized sources. Combining these bioactives into a single delivery system addresses a key stability challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nanoemulsions stabilized by complexes formed between marine protein hydrolysates (e.g., from fish processing by-products) and phenolic-rich extracts from tropical fruit byproducts (e.g., Jerivá or Butiá) will provide superior physical and oxidative stability to encapsulated omega-3 oils, and enhance their bioaccessibility during simulated gastrointestinal digestion.
proposals.method: Protein hydrolysates will be prepared from Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar) frames via enzymatic hydrolysis (Alcalase/Flavourzyme). These will be complexed with phenolic extracts from Jerivá pomace (obtained via solvent extraction and characterized by UPLC-MS/MS for phenolic acid profile). Oil-in-water nanoemulsions will be fabricated using high-pressure homogenization, with the protein-phenolic complexes as emulsifiers and omega-3 rich oil (e.g., menhaden oil) as the core. Systems will be characterized for droplet size, zeta-potential, encapsulation efficiency, and oxidative stability (via PV and TBARS) during storage. In vitro bioaccessibility will be assessed using a simulated gastrointestinal digestion model coupled with micelle fraction analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, clean-label stabilization strategy for omega-3 oils using synergistic marine and plant bioactives, directly valorizing food industry byproducts. It will provide fundamental insights into the structure-function relationship of protein-phenolic complexes in emulsion systems and establish a food-grade nanoencapsulation platform to improve the delivery of oxidatively sensitive lipophilic nutrients.
proposals.novelty: While protein hydrolysates and phenolics have been studied separately as emulsifiers/antioxidants, their pre-complexation as a dedicated, dual-functional (emulsifying + antioxidant) stabilizer for omega-3 nanoemulsions is novel. This integrates Shahidi's expertise in marine hydrolysates, phenolic extraction, and antioxidant evaluation into a cohesive food material science approach for delivery.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating 80% Ethanol for Antioxidant Extraction: A Bioaccessibility-Driven Comparison with Aqueous and Simulated Gastrointestinal Solvents in Marine and Fruit Byproduct Hydrolysates
proposals.motivation: The standard use of 80% ethanol for extracting phenolic antioxidants from food matrices is based on high recovery yields but may not reflect the bioaccessible fraction released during human digestion, which is crucial for assessing true nutraceutical potential. Pre-2026 research by Shahidi and others on marine and fruit byproduct hydrolysates emphasizes bioactive potential, yet the link between extraction solvent and subsequent bioaccessibility remains underexplored. This gap limits the predictive value of in vitro antioxidant assays for in vivo efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: Aqueous-based solvent systems, including simulated gastrointestinal fluids, will extract antioxidant fractions from protein hydrolysates and fruit byproducts that better correlate with in vitro bioaccessibility metrics than the conventional 80% ethanol extract, which may overestimate non-bioavailable compounds.
proposals.method: We will prepare protein hydrolysates from marine sources (e.g., shrimp shell) and extracts from underutilized fruit byproducts (e.g., Jerivá pomace). Antioxidants will be extracted in parallel using: 1) 80% ethanol (control), 2) acidified water (pH ~2), 3) aqueous-enzyme mixtures (e.g., pepsin), and 4) a full simulated gastrointestinal digestion (INFOGEST static model). Extracts will be analyzed for total phenolic content (Folin-Ciocalteu), specific phenolics (UPLC-MS/MS, targeting compounds like gallic acid and pterostilbene), and antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH). Bioaccessibility will be calculated as the antioxidant activity remaining after the simulated digestion of the 80% ethanol extract versus the activity directly extracted by the gastrointestinal solvents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical, evidence-based framework for selecting extraction solvents that better predict the bioaccessible antioxidant activity of food-derived hydrolysates and byproducts. It will directly inform more accurate screening methods for nutraceutical development. The findings will challenge a long-standing laboratory convention and strengthen the link between analytical chemistry and nutritional bioavailability.
proposals.novelty: While solvent optimization studies exist, this proposal uniquely shifts the optimization target from maximal recovery to predictive correlation with bioaccessibility, specifically for protein-rich hydrolysates and complex fruit byproducts. It integrates Shahidi's expertise in marine/fruit phenolics and hydrolysates with bioaccessibility modeling, a combination not commonly applied to challenge this specific extraction standard.

proposals.title: Bioaccessibility and Antioxidant Efficacy of Phenolic-Protein Complexes in Intact Marine Protein Hydrolysates: A Matrix-Based Study
proposals.motivation: Current research on food antioxidants predominantly relies on chemical extracts, which fail to capture the complex interactions within the intact food matrix that govern bioactivity and bioavailability. This is a critical gap, as the health-promoting potential of nutraceuticals like marine-derived peptides and phenolics is likely modulated by their native food environment. A matrix-focused approach is essential to accurately assess the true functional potential of underutilized food sources.
proposals.hypothesis: The antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities of phenolic compounds in marine protein hydrolysates are significantly enhanced and stabilized when assessed within their intact, complex food matrix compared to purified extracts, due to synergistic interactions with peptides and other matrix components.
proposals.method: We will prepare intact, minimally processed hydrolysates from marine byproducts (e.g., shrimp shells, fish frames) using controlled enzymatic hydrolysis. The antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH, FRAP) and cellular anti-inflammatory activity (inhibition of NO production in RAW 264.7 macrophages) of the whole hydrolysate matrix will be compared to its sequentially extracted fractions (phenolic-rich, peptide-rich). Bioaccessibility will be evaluated using a standardized in vitro gastrointestinal digestion model, followed by UPLC-MS/MS to track the stability and transformation of key phenolic-protein complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for evaluating the bioactivity of food components in their native state, moving beyond reductionist extraction. It will deliver concrete evidence on how the marine food matrix acts as a functional delivery system, directly informing the development of more effective whole-food-based nutraceuticals and functional ingredients.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the standard paradigm by using the intact, complex hydrolysate as the primary analytical subject, rather than a starting material for extraction, to directly measure matrix-driven bioactivity. It uniquely integrates bioaccessibility assays with activity measurements on the same matrix, a approach rarely applied to marine protein-phenolic systems.

proposals.title: Differential Impact of Simulated Gastric pH and Pancreatic Enzymes on the Bioaccessibility and Antioxidant Activity of Phenolic Compounds from Wheat Bran Hydrolysates
proposals.motivation: The health benefits of whole grains are linked to phenolic antioxidants, but their bioactivity depends on stability during digestion. While gastric pH is often studied, the specific role of pancreatic enzymes (proteases, amylases, lipases) in modifying phenolic-protein complexes and releasing bound antioxidants from cereal matrices remains unclear. Understanding the primary digestive driver is crucial for designing functional foods and accurate in vitro bioaccessibility models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pancreatic digestive enzymes, rather than low gastric pH, are the primary factor modulating the antioxidant activity of wheat phenolics by hydrolyzing protein-phenolic complexes and cell wall structures, thereby increasing the bioaccessible antioxidant pool.
proposals.method: Wheat bran will be subjected to enzymatic hydrolysis using food-grade proteases (e.g., Alcalase, Flavourzyme) to produce protein-phenolic hydrolysates, mirroring Shahidi's work on marine and fruit byproducts. These hydrolysates will then undergo a sequential, controlled in vitro digestion model where the gastric phase (pH 2.0, pepsin) and intestinal phase (pH 7.0, pancreatin) are applied independently and in combination. Antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH) and phenolic profile (UPLC-MS/MS) will be measured after each stage to isolate the effect of pH versus enzyme action.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the specific digestive mechanism controlling antioxidant release from wheat, moving beyond correlative pH studies. It will provide a refined framework for predicting the nutraceutical potential of cereal-based foods and validate enzymatic pre-treatment as a strategy to enhance the bioactivity of grain-derived ingredients.
proposals.novelty: While pH effects are well-documented, this study systematically isolates the contribution of pancreatic enzyme cocktails on phenolic bioaccessibility, linking Shahidi's expertise in phenolic-protein hydrolysates and antioxidant evaluation directly to a fundamental gap in cereal digestion science.

proposals.title: Comprehensive Gastrointestinal Digestion Modeling to Assess the Stability and Antioxidant Activity of Marine Protein Hydrolysates and Phenolic-Rich Fruit Byproduct Extracts
proposals.motivation: The bioactivity of dietary antioxidants, such as those in marine hydrolysates and fruit byproducts, is often assessed in vitro under simplified conditions that do not reflect the complex, dynamic environment of the human gastrointestinal (GI) tract. Pre-2026 research, including work on gallic acid and tropical fruits, highlights the need to understand how pH, enzymes, and transit time affect bioactive compound stability and function. A full GI model is critical for accurately predicting the nutraceutical potential and health benefits of these food-derived compounds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the antioxidant capacity and phenolic profile of marine protein hydrolysates and phenolic-rich fruit extracts (e.g., from Jerivá or Butiá) will be significantly modified during sequential gastric and intestinal digestion phases, with specific compounds exhibiting differential stability that dictates their final bioactivity.
proposals.method: We will employ a standardized static in vitro digestion model (INFOGEST protocol, pre-2026) to simulate the complete GI tract, including oral, gastric, and intestinal phases with relevant enzymes (pepsin, pancreatin) and bile salts. Marine protein hydrolysates (e.g., from shrimp or fish processing byproducts) and extracts from underutilized tropical fruit byproducts will be subjected to this protocol. Samples will be collected at each phase for analysis of antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH), total phenolic content, and specific phenolic acid profiles (e.g., gallic, p-coumaric acids) via UPLC-MS/MS, comparing digested versus undigested samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, physiologically relevant assessment of the nutraceutical stability of marine and fruit-based antioxidants, directly informing their potential efficacy as functional food ingredients. It will generate novel data on the fate of specific phenolic acids and peptides during digestion, linking processing (extraction/hydrolysis) to final bioactivity. The findings will advance the field of food chemistry by establishing a more predictive framework for evaluating antioxidant sources.
proposals.novelty: While individual digestion phases have been studied, applying a full, standardized GI model to directly compare the digestive fate of antioxidant marine protein hydrolysates and phenolic-rich fruit byproduct extracts—two major themes in Shahidi's recent work—is novel. This integrated approach moves beyond single-phase or single-matrix studies to a holistic, comparative bioaccessibility assessment.

proposals.title: Optimization of Milling and Controlled Thermal Processing to Maximize Phenolic Acid and Antioxidant Retention in Wheat Bran Fractions
proposals.motivation: Wheat bran is a rich but underutilized source of phenolic acids like ferulic acid, offering significant antioxidant and nutraceutical potential. Conventional milling and thermal processing in food engineering often degrade these sensitive bioactive compounds, reducing the health-promoting value of whole-grain ingredients. Applying targeted process engineering to preserve these compounds aligns with the growing demand for functional foods and valorization of agricultural byproducts, a key theme in pre-2026 food science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically engineering milling parameters (e.g., particle size, shear force) and applying controlled, mild thermal treatments, we can significantly enhance the retention and extractability of phenolic acids and the overall antioxidant capacity in processed wheat bran fractions compared to conventional methods.
proposals.method: The study will employ a factorial design to optimize milling (impact vs. shear mills) and thermal (infrared, steam) processes. Wheat bran fractions will be analyzed for phenolic acid profile (specifically ferulic and p-coumaric acids) using UPLC-MS/MS, total phenolic content (Folin-Ciocalteu), and antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH). Protein-phenolic interactions will be assessed via SDS-PAGE and fluorescence spectroscopy. Process parameters will be correlated with bioactivity data using response surface methodology to identify optimal conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated food process engineering framework for maximizing antioxidant retention in cereal processing. It will deliver specific, optimized protocols for producing high-antioxidant wheat bran ingredients, contributing directly to the development of functional foods and nutraceuticals. The findings will expand the application of engineering principles to bioactive preservation, bridging the gap between process design and nutritional quality.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Shahidi's prior work has identified bioactive compounds in various matrices, the novelty here lies in the systematic application and optimization of specific physical engineering unit operations (milling/thermal) explicitly for antioxidant preservation in wheat, a approach distinct from purely chemical extraction studies or biological activity screening.

proposals.title: Computational Prediction of Phenolic Antioxidant Degradation Kinetics from Wheat Hydrolysates Under Simulated Gastrointestinal Conditions
proposals.motivation: The bioaccessibility and stability of dietary antioxidants during digestion are critical for realizing their health-promoting potential, but are difficult and time-consuming to assess experimentally for complex food matrices. Pre-2026, Dr. Shahidi's work on marine and fruit-derived phenolic compounds and protein hydrolysates highlights the need for efficient methods to predict the fate of bioactive compounds. This proposal addresses this gap by focusing on wheat, a major global staple, to model antioxidant stability computationally.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a chemical kinetics model, parameterized with experimental data on key wheat phenolic acids (e.g., ferulic, p-coumaric acid), can accurately predict the degradation profiles of antioxidant activity under varying gastrointestinal pH, enzyme, and transit time conditions.
proposals.method: We will first generate experimental degradation data for wheat bran phenolic extracts and hydrolysates under controlled simulated gastrointestinal phases (oral, gastric, intestinal) using established in vitro protocols. This data will be used to parameterize and validate a system of ordinary differential equations (ODEs) modeling first-order and pseudo-first-order degradation kinetics. The model will be implemented in Python using the SciPy library for ODE integration and optimization (e.g., curve_fit) to determine rate constants. Sensitivity analysis will identify the most critical digestion parameters influencing stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive computational tool to estimate the bioaccessible antioxidant capacity of wheat-based foods, reducing reliance on exhaustive in vitro assays. It will advance the field of food chemistry by integrating chemical kinetics with gastrointestinal simulation, offering a novel framework applicable to other plant-derived antioxidants studied in Dr. Shahidi's lab, such as those from underutilized fruits.
proposals.novelty: While kinetic modeling of food degradation exists, its specific application to predict the stability of wheat antioxidants throughout the multi-phase gastrointestinal tract, informed by Dr. Shahidi's expertise in phenolic profiling and hydrolysates, is novel. The approach moves beyond simple correlation to a mechanistic, condition-dependent prediction model.

proposals.title: Comprehensive Profiling of Antioxidant and Phenolic Compounds in Industrial Cereal Milling Streams: Valorization of High-Value By-Products
proposals.motivation: Industrial cereal milling generates multiple distinct streams beyond the primary flour and bran fractions, many of which are underutilized as low-value feed. Pre-2026 research on food by-products, including marine hydrolysates and tropical fruit waste, demonstrates that such materials can be rich sources of bioactive phenolic compounds and antioxidants. A systematic analysis of *all* commercial milling streams is needed to identify specific, high-potential fractions for nutraceutical and functional food applications, aligning with global trends in circular food economy and waste reduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, commercially produced minor milling streams (e.g., fine brans, middlings, germ-rich fractions) will exhibit significantly higher concentrations of phenolic acids and associated antioxidant capacities compared to major flour streams, making them viable targets for high-value extraction.
proposals.method: We will collect all physical output streams from an industrial wheat and oat milling process. Each stream will be defatted and extracted using optimized aqueous-organic solvent systems. Total phenolic content (Folin-Ciocalteu) and antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH, and ABTS assays) will be quantified. Phenolic acid profiles (e.g., ferulic, p-coumaric, sinapic, gallic acids) and other phenolics will be identified and quantified using UPLC-MS/MS, following methodologies from Shahidi's prior work on fruit by-products and extraction parameter optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of bioactive compound distribution across a full-scale industrial milling operation. It will directly identify the most potent streams for commercial valorization, offering millers a strategy to upgrade by-products. The data will expand the understanding of cereal phytochemicals from a processing perspective, contributing to the fields of food science and nutraceuticals.
proposals.novelty: While the antioxidant potential of major cereal fractions is known, this systematic analysis of *all* industrial co-streams is novel. It applies the detailed phenolic profiling and bioactivity assessment common in fruit by-product research (e.g., Jerivá, Butiá) to the complete, practical output of a core food processing operation, moving beyond laboratory-milled samples.

proposals.title: Targeted Gastric Bioaccessibility: Breeding and Processing Strategies to Engineer Wheat for Optimal Antioxidant Release under Simulated Gastric Conditions
proposals.motivation: The health benefits of dietary antioxidants are contingent on their bioaccessibility during digestion. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Shahidi's work on marine hydrolysates and phenolic compounds, highlights the importance of release kinetics. Wheat is a global staple, but its phenolic acids are largely bound in the insoluble fiber matrix, limiting their antioxidant potential in the upper GI tract where they could exert maximal protective effects. Optimizing release specifically at gastric pH addresses a key bioavailability bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: Select wheat cultivars with distinct protein and phenolic acid profiles, and subject them to targeted enzymatic hydrolysis or fermentation, will exhibit significantly enhanced release of soluble phenolic antioxidants under simulated gastric pH conditions compared to untreated controls.
proposals.method: We will screen diverse wheat genotypes for bound phenolic acid (e.g., ferulic, p-coumaric acid) content and protein composition. Selected cultivars will undergo processing with food-grade proteases and carbohydrases (e.g., xylanases) to hydrolyze the protein-phenolic-carbohydrate matrix. Antioxidant release kinetics will be quantified using in vitro simulated gastric digestion (pH ~2, pepsin). The phenolic profile and antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH) of gastric digesta will be analyzed via UPLC-MS/MS and standard assays. Statistical modeling (e.g., response surface methodology) will identify optimal genotype-process parameter combinations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel 'design-for-digestion' framework for cereal biofortification, moving beyond total phenolic content to functional release. It will identify specific wheat traits and mild processing protocols that maximize gastric bioaccessibility, contributing directly to the development of functional foods with enhanced nutraceutical value. The findings will bridge plant breeding, food chemistry, and nutritional science.
proposals.novelty: While extraction optimization is common, this proposal inverts the problem to design the raw material (wheat) for a specific digestive outcome. It uniquely integrates pre-breeding selection for protein-phenolic matrix characteristics with targeted bioprocessing, a strategy not extensively applied to wheat for gastric-targeted antioxidant delivery, aligning with Shahidi's expertise in marine and fruit byproduct valorization.

proposals.title: A Decision Framework for Selecting Antioxidant Assays: Application to Marine Protein Hydrolysates and Tropical Fruit Byproduct Phenolics
proposals.motivation: The accurate assessment of antioxidant potential is critical for validating the nutraceutical value of food-derived compounds, such as marine protein hydrolysates and phenolic-rich fruit byproducts. However, the proliferation of antioxidant assays (e.g., DPPH, ABTS, ORAC, FRAP, cellular assays) often leads to inconsistent results and inappropriate method selection, hindering reliable comparison and application-specific conclusions. A clear, evidence-based guideline is urgently needed to align assay choice with sample matrix and intended biological or food application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic correlation analysis between chemical antioxidant capacity assays and relevant biological activity endpoints (e.g., inhibition of lipid peroxidation in food systems, cellular antioxidant activity) will reveal distinct clusters of assay performance, enabling the creation of a sample- and application-specific selection framework.
proposals.method: We will analyze a diverse panel of pre-characterized samples from Dr. Shahidi's work, including marine protein hydrolysates and phenolic extracts from tropical fruits (e.g., Jerivá, Butiá) and their byproducts. Each sample will be subjected to a battery of standard chemical antioxidant assays (DPPH, ABTS, FRAP, ORAC, metal chelation) and application-relevant biological/functional tests (e.g., inhibition of β-carotene bleaching, cellular antioxidant activity in HepG2 cells, anti-glycation potential). Statistical analysis, including principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical clustering, will be used to map assay correlations and identify which chemical assays best predict specific functional outcomes for each sample class.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, decision-tree-style guideline for researchers and industry to select the most predictive antioxidant assays based on sample origin (e.g., protein-rich vs. phenolic-rich) and target application (e.g., food stabilization vs. bioactive screening). It will directly enhance the reliability and interpretability of antioxidant claims for underutilized marine and plant resources, supporting their valorization.
proposals.novelty: While many studies compare assays, this proposal uniquely integrates Dr. Shahidi's expertise in marine and fruit byproduct chemistry to develop a pragmatic, sample-type-specific framework, moving beyond generic comparisons to provide actionable selection criteria grounded in correlation with application-relevant functional endpoints.

proposals.title: Bioactive Potential of Marine Byproduct Protein Hydrolysates: Antioxidant, ACE-Inhibitory, and Mineral-Binding Capacities
proposals.motivation: The valorization of marine processing byproducts (e.g., fish frames, shrimp shells) is a critical sustainability challenge in the food industry. These materials are rich in proteins that, upon enzymatic hydrolysis, can release bioactive peptides. Pre-2026 research has established the general bioactivity of hydrolysates, but systematic characterization linking specific hydrolysis conditions to a multi-functional bioactive profile for underutilized marine species remains limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled enzymatic hydrolysis of proteins from selected North Atlantic marine byproducts (e.g., Atlantic cod frames, Northern shrimp shells) will yield hydrolysates with significant and correlated antioxidant, angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE)-inhibitory (antihypertensive), and mineral (iron, calcium)-binding capacities, influenced by the protease type and degree of hydrolysis.
proposals.method: Proteins will be extracted from defatted, dried byproducts. Hydrolysis will be performed using food-grade proteases (Alcalase, Flavourzyme, Bromelain) under optimized pH/temperature conditions, monitoring degree of hydrolysis. Hydrolysates will be fractionated by ultrafiltration (3 kDa, 10 kDa membranes). Bioactivity will be assessed via: 1) Antioxidant capacity (ORAC, DPPH, and FRAP assays), 2) ACE-inhibitory activity in vitro, and 3) Mineral-binding capacity measured by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) after binding assays. Active fractions will be analyzed for peptide sequences using UPLC-ESI-MS/MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comparative multi-activity profile for hydrolysates from specific, regionally relevant marine byproducts, directly supporting their use as functional food ingredients. It will establish correlations between hydrolysis parameters, peptide profiles, and multi-functional bioactivity, offering practical guidance for industry. The data will contribute to a circular bioeconomy by transforming waste into high-value nutraceuticals.
proposals.novelty: While Shahidi's group has studied marine hydrolysates and phenolic antioxidants separately, this proposal integrates the assessment of antioxidant, antihypertensive, and mineral-binding bioactivities within a single, systematic framework for specific underutilized North Atlantic species, linking bioactivity directly to hydrolysis process variables and peptide characteristics.

ideas.title: Thermo-Resilient Antioxidant Extraction and Stability Assessment from Food By-Products
ideas.description: This research develops and validates extraction protocols for phenolic antioxidants from food industry by-products (e.g., fruit pomace, seed cakes) that are explicitly designed to maximize not only yield but also the thermo-resilience of the extracted compounds. The extracted antioxidants are then systematically evaluated under dynamic, non-isothermal storage conditions that mimic real supply chain and consumer storage scenarios. This integrated approach identifies optimal extraction parameters (solvent, time, method) that co-optimize for both high recovery and enhanced stability under thermal stress, providing a direct link between sustainable sourcing and functional performance for commercial application. The outcome is a targeted methodology to upcycle waste streams into high-value, robust antioxidant ingredients with predictable shelf-life performance.

ideas.title: Investigate the role of interfacial rheology in modulating the bioaccessibility and antioxidant activity of dietary phenolics in emulsions
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how the interfacial rheological properties imparted by dietary phenolic extracts at oil-water interfaces in food emulsions govern not only physical stability but also the specific in vivo fate of the phenolics themselves. By correlating interfacial viscoelasticity with the controlled release, bioaccessibility, and subsequent metabolic processing of the antioxidants during simulated digestion, the study aims to establish a mechanistic link between colloidal structure and biological function. This approach allows for the rational design of functional emulsions where the interfacial architecture is engineered to optimize both shelf-life and targeted health effects, such as scavenging radicals in specific digestive compartments or modulating uptake pathways.

ideas.title: Adapting Bioleaching from Mining to Optimize Phenolic Extraction from Food Residues
ideas.description: This research idea proposes using an analogical solution from the field of biomining/metallurgy, where bioleaching uses microorganisms to extract valuable metals from low-grade ores. The analogous solution involves employing specific microbes or their enzymatic by-products to solubilize target compounds from complex, residual matrices. Adapted to the food context, the idea is to develop efficient extraction methods for phenolic antioxidants by engineering or applying tailored microbial consortia and their enzymes to pre-treat food industry by-products. This biological pre-treatment would selectively degrade lignocellulosic and other structural components that bind phenolics, thereby optimizing their release and subsequent recovery using environmentally friendly solvents, enhancing both yield and commercial viability.

ideas.title: Dynamic Phenolic Degradation: Mapping the Non-Linear Relationship Between Fortification Level, Temperature Fluctuations, and Final Food Quality
ideas.description: This research investigates the non-linear and time-dependent relationship between phenolic compound levels and overall food quality under dynamic, real-world storage conditions. It will determine how fluctuating temperatures catalyze the degradation of added phenolics into specific compounds that directly cause negative sensory attributes (e.g., off-flavors, bitterness) and antagonistic nutrient interactions. The outcome will be a time-temperature-fortification matrix that identifies optimal phenolic ranges and stabilization strategies for specific food matrices, ensuring fortified products maintain their intended health and sensory benefits throughout a realistic supply chain.

ideas.title: Integrated biorefinery platform for stabilized, application-ready phenolic ingredients from agri-food residues
ideas.description: This research develops a holistic, circular biorefinery platform that moves beyond mere extraction to deliver commercially viable phenolic ingredients. The core innovation is the tight coupling of targeted extraction (using tunable green solvents) with in-line stabilization and formulation technologies, such as encapsulation onto food-grade carriers, to prevent degradation and standardize bioactivity. A continuous techno-economic and environmental assessment model guides process parameter optimization in real-time, ensuring the final ingredient modules (e.g., powders for baking, oils for cosmetics) meet strict cost, stability, and functional specifications for end-use industries. The outcome is a robust, economically defensible pipeline that transforms variable by-products into consistent, high-value antioxidant ingredients with guaranteed performance.

ideas.title: Stimuli-responsive nano-encapsulation for targeted intestinal delivery of phenolic antioxidants
ideas.description: This research designs and validates core-shell nano-delivery systems using materials engineered for targeted, stimuli-responsive release. The encapsulation matrix combines food-grade polymers (e.g., alginate, chitosan, zein) that are stable during thermal and mechanical food processing but degrade at specific pH thresholds or via enzymatic action in the gastrointestinal tract. The approach moves beyond mere stability to actively control the release kinetics and location, ensuring phenolic antioxidants are protected until they reach their optimal site for absorption or action. This significantly enhances the documented bioavailability and functional health impact of the delivered bioactives compared to simple protective encapsulation.

ideas.title: Risk-Prioritized Toxicology of Processing-Induced Phenolic Degradants: From Predictive Screening to Targeted Hazard Assessment
ideas.description: This research establishes a closed-loop system to identify and evaluate the most toxicologically relevant degradation products of natural phenolics. It begins by simulating extreme, real-world food processing conditions to generate defined chemical libraries of degradants. These libraries are then computationally and experimentally screened using high-throughput in vitro toxicity assays to prioritize compounds of greatest concern. Finally, the high-priority degradants undergo systematic, mechanism-focused toxicological evaluation, creating a decision-making framework that links processing parameters to potential chemical hazards.

ideas.title: Apply ecological predator-prey modeling to predict in vivo antioxidant dose-response
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Theoretical Ecology to the original problem of predicting in vivo antioxidant dose-response. In ecology, the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model is a classic tool for simulating the dynamic, non-linear interaction between two competing populations. This analogous solution is adapted by conceptualizing dietary phenolic antioxidants as the 'predator' population and reactive oxygen species (ROS) as the 'prey' population within a biological compartment (e.g., plasma). The adapted research idea involves developing a pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK-PD) model where the absorption and distribution (PK) of the phenolic compound feeds into a dynamic systems model (PD). This model would simulate the 'scavenging' interaction, incorporating parameters for the endogenous generation rate of ROS and the phenolic compound's reaction kinetics. This approach provides a mechanistic framework to predict the time-course and efficacy of antioxidant interventions, capturing potential rebound effects or non-linear responses that static models miss.

ideas.title: A High-Throughput Screening Platform for Antioxidant Performance in Complex Matrices, Inspired by Drug Discovery
ideas.description: This research adapts the high-throughput screening (HTS) methodology from pharmaceutical drug discovery to solve the comparative problem in food science. In drug discovery, HTS platforms rapidly evaluate vast libraries of natural and synthetic compounds for efficacy and safety. The adapted idea is to create an analogous, automated screening platform specifically for antioxidants. This system would simultaneously test numerous natural phenolics and synthetic alternatives under varied, controlled conditions mimicking real food matrices (e.g., emulsions, acidic beverages, baked goods). By employing robotic liquid handling, a battery of chemical and cellular antioxidant assays, and real-time stability monitoring, the platform would generate comprehensive, comparative datasets on efficacy, degradation kinetics, and matrix interactions. This data-driven approach would move beyond pairwise comparisons to enable the rational, application-specific selection of optimal antioxidants based on a multi-parameter performance profile, directly addressing the core challenges of comparative efficacy, stability, and cost-effectiveness.

ideas.title: Rational Design of Clean-Label Emulsifiers via Interfacial Rheology-Guided Extraction of Phenolics from Food By-Products
ideas.description: This research program integrates the extraction of phenolic antioxidants from food by-products with advanced interfacial rheology from soft matter physics to rationally design next-generation, clean-label emulsifiers. The core innovation is using interfacial rheological measurements (e.g., dilatational and shear moduli) as a diagnostic feedback mechanism to iteratively refine the extraction parameters (solvent, pH, temperature) to isolate phenolic fractions with optimal surface-active and film-strengthening properties. This approach moves beyond simply characterizing extracted materials to actively engineering their functionality, creating a direct link between extraction chemistry, interfacial material science, and final emulsion stability. The outcome is a sustainable, data-driven methodology for producing high-value, multifunctional ingredients (antioxidant + emulsifier) from waste streams with predictable and superior performance in complex food systems.

ideas.title: Chemometric-Guided Optimization and Authentication of Antioxidant Extracts from Thyme Distillation Waste
ideas.description: This research fuses chemometric pattern recognition with the optimization of extraction from deodorized thyme waste. The core innovation is using chemometric analysis (e.g., PCA, PLS-DA) on spectroscopic or chromatographic data of initial waste samples to identify key source-specific variables that predict high antioxidant yield and profile. This fingerprint data then directly informs and steers the selection of solvents and extraction methods, moving beyond simple yield maximization to target a specific, authenticable chemical signature. The final output is a validated, optimized extraction protocol that consistently produces a standardized, traceable antioxidant extract from an industrial by-product, with built-in chemometric models for ongoing quality control and origin verification.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Food Matrices as Phenolic Delivery Vehicles via Pharmaceutical Formulation Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the core problem-solving approach from pharmaceutical drug delivery to food design. In pharmaceuticals, the inverse problem of designing a delivery system for a specific drug's targeted release is solved using principles of material science and biophysics to create layered, composite formulations. We propose to apply this analogical framework to food by reverse-engineering food matrices as precision delivery vehicles for phenolic antioxidants. The adapted idea is to treat phenolics as active ingredients and use food-grade biopolymers and structures (like emulsion-filled gels, coated particles, or structured fibers) to program their stability, bioavailability, and site-specific release within the gastrointestinal tract, thereby optimizing their intended health benefits based on the phenolic's specific target site and mechanism of action.

ideas.title: Inverted Stability Testing via Corrosion Inhibition Analogy
ideas.description: This idea inverts the typical oil blend ratio premise by drawing an analogy from corrosion science. In metallurgy, testing a corrosion inhibitor's efficacy begins with a pure, reactive metal in a harsh environment, not a pre-alloyed or partially protected one. Adapted to lipid research, the experiment starts with oxidation-prone pure wheat germ oil to create a high-stress baseline. The protective efficacy of a single antioxidant extract is tested thoroughly in this unstable, pure medium. Only after this primary mechanism is quantified is almond oil—a more stable secondary component—added incrementally. This method isolates the extract's true performance from the confounding stabilizing effects of a pre-mixed blend, potentially revealing non-linear synergies or identifying minimum effective concentrations more accurately.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic (PK-PD) Modeling to Map Dietary Phenolic Bioactivity
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a parallel problem in clinical pharmacology—understanding the complex in vivo fate and action of synthetic compounds—to the study of dietary phenolics. In drug development, integrated Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic (PK-PD) modeling is used to mathematically link a compound's concentration-time profile in the body to the time course and magnitude of its biological effects. The adapted idea is to apply this rigorous PK-PD modeling framework to human studies on dietary phenolics. Researchers would conduct detailed, timed human intervention studies, treating specific phenolic compounds or their definitive metabolites as the active agents. By simultaneously generating rich pharmacokinetic data (via advanced analytics like LC-MS/MS) and high-frequency, dynamic pharmacodynamic data on relevant antioxidant mechanisms and health biomarkers, they can build mathematical models. These models would clarify whether observed health effects are direct consequences of circulating parent compounds, are mediated by specific metabolites, exhibit time delays (e.g., due to gene expression changes), or result from indirect adaptive responses, thereby moving beyond correlative associations to mechanistic, quantitative predictions of phenolic bioactivity in vivo.

ideas.title: Chemometric-Guided Metabolic Flux Analysis for Bioactive Standardization
ideas.description: This research direction integrates chemometric pattern recognition with metabolic flux analysis to establish a new paradigm for standardizing natural antioxidant extracts. Chemometrics is used to create detailed chemical fingerprints of extracts from varying botanical sources and processing methods. These fingerprints are then used as input constraints for genome-scale metabolic models to predict their specific effects on cellular antioxidant and redox pathway fluxes. The resulting synergy allows for the development of efficacy-based standards, where extracts are authenticated and standardized not just by chemical markers, but by their predicted, quantifiable impact on core cellular metabolic functions.

ideas.title: Adapting Vaccine Adjuvant Delivery for Antioxidant Encapsulation
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from vaccinology and immunotherapy, where lipid and polymer nanoparticles are engineered to protect fragile biological cargo (like antigens or mRNA) from degradation and control its delivery. The analogous solution involves designing carriers with specific release triggers, such as pH-sensitive or enzyme-cleavable linkers, to ensure cargo stability until it reaches the target site. Adapted to materials science for food applications, the research would develop nano-encapsulation systems for phenolic antioxidants using similar trigger-responsive materials (e.g., Eudragit polymers or specific lipid compositions). These capsules would be designed to withstand the harsh conditions of food processing (heat, shear, neutral pH) but would degrade in response to the specific pH or enzymatic environment of the human small intestine, thereby ensuring the antioxidants are protected until the point of optimal bioavailability and release.

ideas.title: Establish a tiered, context-specific framework for antioxidant activity measurement
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecotoxicology, which solved the problem of assessing chemical toxicity across diverse environmental contexts by establishing tiered, standardized testing guidelines (e.g., OECD frameworks), this research idea adapts that solution to the measurement of antioxidant activity. The goal is to establish a standardized, multi-tiered framework for measuring and reporting the antioxidant activity of phenolic compounds. Tier 1 would consist of validated, high-throughput chemical assays (e.g., ORAC, FRAP, DPPH) with strict protocol definitions for basic comparison. Tier 2 would involve more biologically relevant assays, such as cell-based models of oxidative stress or biomarker measurements in specific in vivo models, tailored to particular research questions (e.g., neuroprotection, cardiovascular health). This framework would mandate clear reporting of the tier, specific assay, and experimental conditions, finally allowing for consistent, comparable, and biologically interpretable data across the field.

ideas.title: Kinetic model-informed design of predictive non-isothermal stability tests for antioxidants
ideas.description: This research first quantifies the fundamental temperature-dependent degradation kinetics of specific antioxidants and their substrates under controlled isothermal conditions. Using the derived Arrhenius parameters, the study then designs fluctuating temperature protocols that are 'kinetically equivalent' to realistic storage or transport profiles, meaning they are predicted to cause the same net chemical degradation. The antioxidant performance is then experimentally tested under these designed non-isothermal cycles, and the results are compared to the model's predictions and to outcomes from real-world scenario simulations. This approach replaces arbitrary fluctuation with a mechanistic, predictive framework that can generalize performance to untested temperature histories.

ideas.description: This research direction integrates advanced nano-encapsulation systems from materials science with high-resolution, single-cell biosensing to create a novel analytical platform. It aims to design and test 'smart' nanocarriers that not only protect phenolic antioxidants during processing but also control their intracellular release in response to specific oxidative stress signals. By using real-time, single-cell imaging of antioxidant response (e.g., via genetically encoded redox sensors), the project will directly correlate nanomaterial parameters—like shell composition, degradation rate, and surface functionalization—with the kinetics, localization, and efficacy of phenolic activity at the subcellular level. This provides a foundational tool for engineering next-generation delivery systems where the material is co-designed with its precise biological function in a living system.

ideas.title: Predictive modeling of solvent efficacy based on physicochemical characterization of thyme distillation waste
ideas.description: This research first performs a detailed physicochemical characterization of deodorized thyme waste to quantify the structural and compositional changes induced by the distillation process. It then correlates these material properties with the experimental extraction efficiency of various solvents for different residual bioactive compound classes using multivariate chemometric analysis. The core output is a predictive model that can recommend optimal solvent-system parameters for a given waste batch based on its measurable characteristics. This mechanistic approach transforms the process from empirical trial-and-error to a science-guided strategy with generalizable principles for biomass valorization.

ideas.title: Apply Chemometric Modeling from Metabolomics, Adapted via Geochemical Fingerprinting
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from petroleum geochemistry, where chemometric models link complex hydrocarbon fingerprints to reservoir properties. The analogous problem of linking a multi-component chemical profile to a functional outcome is solved there using multivariate regression on chromatographic data. Adapted to this context, the research will use multivariate analysis (e.g., PLS-R) to treat the phenolic acid chromatographic profile from plant extracts as a defining chemical fingerprint. The model will be trained to predict quantitative antioxidant performance metrics based on specific patterns and concentrations within this fingerprint, enabling the prediction of extract efficacy from chemical composition alone.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Molecule FRET for Single-Cell Redox Sensing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from structural biology—single-molecule FRET (smFRET) used to measure real-time conformational dynamics—to the problem of measuring antioxidant effects in single cells. The analogous solution monitors energy transfer between fluorophores to detect angstrom-scale distance changes in individual biomolecules. Adapted here, a genetically encoded, redox-sensitive FRET biosensor would be targeted to core antioxidant system components (e.g., the Keap1-Nrf2 complex). By introducing specific phenolics to a single living cell under oxidative stress, real-time changes in FRET efficiency would report the direct, immediate effect of the phenolic on the conformational state and activity of the antioxidant machinery, providing unprecedented single-cell resolution of phenolic bioactivity.

ideas.title: Sequential Multi-Solvent Extraction of Bioactives from Thyme Waste Using a Biomimetic Solvent Gradient
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from proteomics and chromatography—the use of sequential solvent gradients to separate complex mixtures—to optimize bioactive compound extraction from deodorized thyme waste. The analogous problem of maximizing recovery of diverse compounds from a single, complex biological sample is solved in proteomics via multi-step elution using solvents of varying polarity. The adapted idea is to investigate a sequential multi-solvent extraction protocol for thyme waste, employing a carefully designed gradient (e.g., from non-polar hexane to polar water) to selectively target and recover different bioactive compound classes in high-purity fractions. This approach aims to surpass the yield of single-solvent methods, provide a more comprehensive valorization of the waste stream, and generate fractionated extracts tailored for specific applications in food, cosmetic, or pharmaceutical industries.

ideas.title: Continuous Counter-Current Extraction for Thyme Waste Antioxidants
ideas.description: This research adapts the continuous counter-current leaching process from hydrometallurgy—where it is used to extract metals from low-grade ores—to scale up antioxidant extraction from thyme distillation waste. The analogous solution uses a multi-stage system where the extracting solvent flows opposite to the solid feedstock, maximizing the concentration gradient for efficient mass transfer. The adapted idea involves designing a continuous, food-grade counter-current extraction process to handle large volumes of waste, producing a concentrated antioxidant stream with high yield and reduced solvent consumption, enabling viable industrial application.

ideas.title: Adapting Forensic Soil Provenance Methods for Botanical Extract Authentication
ideas.description: This idea adapts the proven chemometric methods from forensic soil provenance analysis to solve the problem of authenticating and standardizing natural antioxidant extracts. In forensic geoscience, a soil sample's origin is determined by applying multivariate pattern recognition (e.g., PCA, PLS-DA, SIMCA) to its multi-elemental and mineralogical signature, creating a unique fingerprint traceable to a specific location. This solution is adapted by constructing analogous multivariate fingerprints for botanical extracts using their full phytochemical profiles obtained via analytical techniques like HPLC, GC-MS, and NMR. The resulting model would authenticate extracts by matching their complex chemical pattern to a curated database of fingerprints from verified sources, thereby standardizing identification based on intrinsic chemical markers rather than a few target compounds, mitigating fraud and ensuring provenance and batch-to-batch consistency.

ideas.title: Adaptive Chemometric Fingerprinting with Natural Variability Integration for Robust Antioxidant Authentication
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond static chemometric pattern matching to create a dynamic, adaptive authentication system for natural antioxidant extracts. It explicitly models and incorporates the known ranges of natural chemical variation caused by geographic, seasonal, and genetic factors into its recognition algorithms, using large, temporally and spatially diverse reference libraries. The system employs robust one-class classification and anomaly detection methods to define the acceptable chemometric 'envelope' for authentic materials, significantly reducing false negatives. Furthermore, it includes a framework for continuous model refinement with new validated production data, ensuring long-term reliability and practical utility for industrial standardization.

ideas.title: Adaptive Feedstock Harmonization for Robust Industrial-Scale Phenolic Biorefining
ideas.description: This redesigned system prioritizes overcoming feedstock variability as the primary barrier to scale. It employs a modular preprocessing hub equipped with sensors and rapid analytics to continuously characterize incoming agricultural and food waste. A central process controller uses this data to dynamically adjust blending ratios and select from a suite of physical, chemical, or enzymatic pre-treatment modules to standardize the feedstock's phenolic profile and accessibility before it enters the extraction biorefinery. This approach guarantees consistent product quality and yield, transforming waste variability from a fatal flaw into a managed input parameter for economically robust, industrial-scale valorization.

ideas.title: Probabilistic, Microbiome-Aware Design of Food Matrices for Reliable Phenolic Delivery
ideas.description: This research system addresses the core failure mode of inter-individual variability by designing food matrices for predictable phenolic delivery outcomes across diverse human gut microbiomes. It employs high-throughput, multi-donor in vitro gut models to generate a vast dataset linking matrix designs to phenolic metabolism profiles under different microbial communities. Machine learning models trained on this and paired host-microbiome omics data then identify robust design principles that maximize the probability of target delivery despite microbial uncertainty. The output is a probabilistic design framework and a library of context-specific food prototypes, validated for efficacy in stratified human cohorts, ensuring reliable bioactivity.

ideas.title: Apply Traffic Flow Network Analysis to Map Phenolic-Induced Redox Pathway Flux
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from urban traffic flow network analysis to the problem of mapping how dietary phenolics alter cellular antioxidant pathways. In traffic engineering, sensors and mathematical models quantify vehicle flux to identify bottlenecks and predict system behavior after perturbations. By analogizing metabolic pathways to a road network and isotopic tracers to traffic sensors, we can apply similar network flow algorithms to construct a dynamic, quantitative flux map. This adapted method will identify phenolic-induced 'bottlenecks' or 'new routes' in redox pathways, modeling the rerouting of electron flow and antioxidant resource allocation to predict the systemic cellular consequences of specific dietary compounds.

ideas.title: Phenomic Intervention Evidence Lattice (PIEL): A multi-scale causal inference framework for dietary phenolics
ideas.description: This system establishes a coordinated network of smaller, high-precision trials (N-of-1 to moderate-N) to measure acute and mid-term effects of specific phenolic interventions on validated, disease-relevant mechanistic biomarkers (e.g., inflammatory markers, endothelial function). These trials use highly controlled diets or purified supplements. Concurrently, it leverages large, existing population cohorts with detailed dietary data, bio-banks, and health records, but standardizes a core panel of the same mechanistic biomarkers in a subset of participants. Advanced causal inference models (e.g., target trial emulation, Mendelian randomization with phenolic-related genetic variants) are then applied to the observational data, using the mechanistic trial results to inform and constrain the models, thereby triangulating evidence and estimating long-term disease risk reduction without a single, impossible mega-trial.

ideas.title: Correlate Interfacial Rheology and Microstructure with Accelerated Stability Testing
ideas.description: This redesigned approach systematically links the macroscopic mechanical properties of the oil-water interface, as measured by interfacial rheology, with direct visualization of the interfacial microstructure formed by the extract. It then subjects the resulting emulsions to controlled stress tests to establish a predictive relationship between the fundamental interfacial measurements and the emulsion's practical stability against coalescence and Ostwald ripening. This multi-scale methodology identifies not just if the extract stabilizes an interface, but how specific interfacial structures contribute to long-term stability. The outcome is a robust, mechanistic explanation for emulsion stabilization that connects molecular composition to interfacial mechanics, microstructure, and functional performance.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Precision Public Health' Model from Infectious Disease Surveillance to Phenolic Intervention Trials
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from infectious disease epidemiology, where integrated surveillance systems enable large-scale evaluation of public health interventions. The analogous problem is scaling the measurement of an exposure (pathogen vs. phenolic compound) and its effect on population health outcomes. The adapted solution is to create a 'Phenolic Intervention Surveillance Network' that combines passive, large-scale data collection (e.g., from linked electronic health records, loyalty card purchasing data, and national mortality/morbidity registries) with active, high-fidelity data from a smaller network of sentinel research centers. The sentinel centers provide gold-standard dietary assessment and biospecimen analysis to validate and calibrate the population-level exposure data. This hybrid design allows for the long-term, population-level trial of phenolic consumption's impact on disease incidence at a scale and cost that a traditional clinical trial could not achieve, mirroring how public health agencies monitor vaccine effectiveness across entire nations.

ideas.title: Adapt High-Throughput Cell Sorting for Emulsion Stability Screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts the high-throughput screening principle of Fluorescence-Activated Cell Sorting (FACS) from biomedical engineering to the problem of testing extract efficacy for emulsion stability. The analogous solution uses microfluidics to generate, interrogate, and sort individual cells based on optical markers. Adapted here, a microfluidic platform generates libraries of monodisperse emulsion droplets, each encapsulating a different test extract and a fluorescent molecular reporter sensitive to a specific instability pathway (e.g., oxidation, coalescence). Droplets are aged on-chip in a delay line and then pass through a laser interrogation point where fluorescence is measured as a proxy for stability. A downstream sorting mechanism (e.g., dielectrophoretic or pneumatic) physically separates droplets into stable and unstable populations based on a predefined fluorescence threshold, enabling rapid, quantitative screening and direct recovery of the most promising extracts.

ideas.title: Microfluidic Emulsion Stability Screening with Integrated Dynamic Stress and Aging Analysis
ideas.description: This approach combines high-throughput generation of monodisperse emulsion libraries with a programmable post-formation stressing module that mimics real-world destabilization processes. Droplets are exposed to controlled shear, temperature gradients, and extended incubation periods within the microfluidic platform itself. In-line optical monitoring tracks droplet size distribution, count, and phase separation over time under these stresses. This provides direct, predictive data on extract efficacy not just in forming emulsions, but in maintaining their stability against relevant failure modes.

ideas.title: Adapting Photoresist Nano-Patterning for Controlled Nanoemulsion Assembly
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of incorporating waste extract into food-grade nanoemulsions by drawing an analogy from semiconductor nanofabrication. The analogous problem in semiconductor lithography is creating precise, nanoscale patterns in a polymer film (photoresist) using controlled energy exposure (light/electron beams) and development. The adapted solution applies this 'patterning' concept to the self-assembly process of nanoemulsions. By using controlled, sequential energy inputs (e.g., specific shear, ultrasonication, or thermal cycles) during emulsification, we can 'pattern' the interfacial layer and internal phase of the nanoemulsion droplet. This creates structurally complex, non-homogeneous droplets where the waste extract components are strategically located—for instance, concentrated in a protected core or arranged in a gradient towards the interface. This engineered architecture aims to significantly enhance the controlled release, targeted delivery, and environmental stability of the bioactives from the waste extract, moving beyond simple homogeneous dispersion to functionally designed nano-carriers.

ideas.title: Adapt Interfacial Microrheology from Pulmonary Surfactant Research
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from pulmonary biophysics, where the mechanical stabilization of the air-liquid interface by surfactant films is critically measured using interfacial microrheology. The analogous problem—understanding how a complex mixture stabilizes a fragile interface against collapse—is solved by applying precise oscillatory shear and compression to the interface and measuring its viscoelastic response. Adapted to the original soft matter context, this involves using interfacial rheometry to measure the extract's effect on the oil-water interface's viscoelastic moduli. This provides a direct mechanical explanation for emulsion stabilization, showing how the extract forms a robust, gel-like layer that resists droplet coalescence, analogous to how pulmonary surfactants prevent alveolar collapse.
